# Locality Guide: Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania (1840s–1860s)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Formed:** March 1, 1811, from Berks and Northampton counties
- **County seat:** Pottsville
- **Population during period:** ~29,000 (1840) → ~60,700 (1850) → ~89,500 (1860). The county nearly tripled in two decades — one of the fastest-growing in Pennsylvania.
- **Economy:** Anthracite coal mining dominated; secondary industries included iron manufacturing, agriculture in the valleys, and commerce along the Schuylkill Canal and Reading Railroad.
- **Ethnic composition:** Welsh (early miners), English, Irish (massive influx post-1847 Famine — the largest immigrant group by the late 1850s), Germans (especially 1848 political refugees), and Scots.
- **Religious denominations:** Welsh Calvinist Methodist, Presbyterian, Methodist Episcopal, Episcopal, Roman Catholic (Irish and German), Lutheran, Baptist, German Reformed. Many congregations conducted services in Welsh, German, or Latin.
- **Key historical events:** Rapid industrialization of the anthracite fields; large-scale Famine Irish immigration (1847–1855); Civil War mobilization (1861–1865), including the celebrated 48th Pennsylvania Infantry; early labor unrest preceding the Molly Maguires.

## Boundary changes

Schuylkill County's boundaries were **stable throughout the 1840s–1860s**. Any records for events before 1811 in what is now Schuylkill County will be in **Berks County** (to the south) or **Northampton County** (to the east).

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)
- **Start date:** Pennsylvania did **not** require statewide civil registration until **1906** (births/deaths) and **1885** (marriages). No civil vital registration existed in the 1840s–1860s.
- **What exists for this period:** Essentially nothing in the civil record. A handful of townships may have kept informal local registers, but these are exceptions.
- **Primary substitute:** Church records are the **only systematic source** for vital events in this period. See Church Records below.
- **Digitization level:** Not applicable — no civil registration system existed.

### Church records
This is the single most important record category for this place and period.

- **Denominations present:** Welsh Calvinist Methodist, Presbyterian, Methodist Episcopal, Episcopal, Roman Catholic, Lutheran, Baptist, German Reformed, and others.
- **Record types:** Baptism/christening registers, marriage registers, burial records, confirmation lists, vestry minutes, communicant rolls.
- **Language:** English (most denominations); **Welsh** (Calvinist Methodist chapels); **German** (Lutheran, German Reformed, some Catholic); **Latin** (some early Catholic registers).
- **FamilySearch holdings:**
  - **Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985** (Collection 1401638): 1,245,830 records · 982,415 persons · 607,220 images. This is the single most important FamilySearch collection for this period. → **Indexed + images** (free).
  - **Lutheran Church Records, Schuylkill County, 1840–1875** (image group 004288771): 198 images in English and German. **0% name-indexed; not fulltext-searchable.** Must be browsed image by image. → **Browse-only images** (free).
- **Where else held:** Schuylkill County Historical Society (microfilm and some originals); Diocese of Allentown Archives (Roman Catholic parishes); Presbyterian Historical Society, Philadelphia; Historical Society of Pennsylvania (many Welsh chapel records); ELCA Archives (Lutheran).
- **Research tip:** For Irish Catholic families, identify the specific parish by township. The earliest Schuylkill County Catholic parishes include St. Patrick's, Pottsville (est. 1827) and St. Clair. Welsh families should seek Welsh Calvinist Methodist chapel records — some are in Welsh and on FamilySearch microfilm.

### Census records
- **Federal census years:** 1840, 1850, 1860, 1870 — all cover Schuylkill County.
- **1840:** Head of household only; all others counted by age/sex category.
- **1850 & 1860:** Name every household member with age, birthplace, and occupation. Both are accompanied by **Mortality Schedules** (deaths in the preceding year, with cause of death and birthplace). The 1860 census also includes Agriculture and Industry Schedules.
- **Pennsylvania state census:** Pennsylvania did **not** conduct a state census in this period.
- **Access (FamilySearch):** The 1850–1860 Schuylkill County census (image group 007621224, 412 images) is **96% name-indexed and fulltext-searchable** — the most accessible record type in this guide. → **Indexed + images** (free). The Ancestry collection "1850 United States Federal Census – Schuylkill County" (collection 1276) provides an additional indexed access point.
- **Caution:** The 1890 federal census was almost entirely destroyed in a 1921 fire; surviving Pennsylvania veterans' schedules are on FamilySearch but cover only a fraction of the population.

### Probate and court records
- **Types:** Wills, letters of administration, estate inventories, guardianship records, Orphans' Court proceedings.
- **FamilySearch holding:** Probate Records, Schuylkill County, 1845–1880 (image group 004812990): 286 images. **Only 4% name-indexed, but fulltext-searchable.** Use FamilySearch's fulltext search to find names in the volume; do not rely on the name index alone. → **Mostly browse-only; fulltext-searchable**.
- **Where held (originals):** Register of Wills / Orphans' Court, Schuylkill County Courthouse, Pottsville. The courthouse has not suffered a major fire, so records should be substantially intact from 1811 forward.
- **Research value:** Estate inventories name heirs and relationships; guardianship proceedings name minor children with ages; administrators' accounts identify neighbors and creditors — all substituting for missing vital records.

### Land records
- **System:** Pennsylvania is a **state-land state** using metes-and-bounds survey. There are no BLM/General Land Office patents for Pennsylvania.
- **Types:** Deed books (grantee/grantor), mortgages, warrants and surveys (original Penn grants), tax records.
- **Research note for this period:** The anthracite boom generated an extremely high volume of land transactions as mining companies bought out small landowners throughout the 1840s–1860s. Deed records frequently document family land holdings and the terms of their transfer.
- **Where held:** Schuylkill County Recorder of Deeds, Pottsville (deed books); Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg (original land warrants and surveys from Penn's Land Office); FamilySearch Catalog (microfilm of many deed books — search "Schuylkill, Pennsylvania, United States – Land and Property").
- **Digitization level:** Microfilm / physical; limited online indexing.

### Military records
- **Mexican-American War (1846–1848):** Pennsylvania volunteer companies; service records at NARA.
- **Civil War (1861–1865):** Schuylkill County contributed multiple regiments. The **48th Pennsylvania Infantry** (the famous "Miners' Regiment," organizers of the crater mine at Petersburg) is extensively documented. Other units include the 76th, 96th, and 129th Pennsylvania Infantry.
- **Record types:**
  - Federal service records and pension files: NARA (accessible on Fold3/Ancestry). Pension files are particularly rich — they often include birth dates, marriage dates, children's names, and physical descriptions.
  - Pennsylvania adjutant general records: Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg (muster rolls, casualty lists, regimental histories).
  - Civil War draft records (1863 Enrollment Act): NARA; indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch.
  - Bounty land warrants: For veterans of wars through 1855 (Mexican War qualifies); NARA.
- **Digitization level:** Indexed + images for most (Ancestry/Fold3); physical at NARA for undigitized original files.

### Cemetery records
- **Major cemeteries:** Charles Baber Cemetery, Pottsville (est. 1828, nonsectarian); numerous church graveyards; small patch-town miners' cemeteries.
- **Online coverage:** **FindAGrave** has substantial Schuylkill County coverage — the primary online resource. FamilySearch links to FindAGrave records.
- **Physical records:** Sexton records and burial registers at individual churches and the Schuylkill County Historical Society; not comprehensively digitized.
- **Research caution:** Many early mining-era burials are in small patch-town cemeteries that are not on FindAGrave. The Schuylkill County Historical Society's cemetery files are essential.
- **Digitization level:** Partial — FindAGrave indexed; original sexton records physical only.

### Newspaper records
- **The Miners' Journal** (Pottsville, founded 1825): The single most valuable local newspaper for Schuylkill County. Obituaries regularly name surviving family members, birthplace, and — uniquely valuable for immigrant research — the county of origin in Ireland, Wales, or Germany. Also contains marriage notices, legal notices, mining accident reports, and court proceedings.
- **Other papers:** Pottsville Emporium, Schuylkill Haven Gazette, various borough papers.
- **Where held:** Pottsville Free Public Library (extensive Miners' Journal microfilm); Schuylkill County Historical Society. Partial coverage on Newspapers.com and GenealogyBank (subscription); limited Pennsylvania coverage on Chronicling America (free, Library of Congress).
- **Digitization level:** Partial microfilm; limited fulltext-searchable online. Contact Pottsville Free Public Library directly.

### Immigration and naturalization records
- **Context:** Schuylkill County had one of the largest immigrant populations in Pennsylvania during this period — the naturalization records are a **high-priority source**.
- **Ports of entry:** Philadelphia (primary), Baltimore, New York. Passenger lists for all three are indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry and name every person aboard, including infants traveling with parents.
- **Naturalization records:** Before 1906, any court of record could process naturalizations. In Schuylkill County, the **Court of Common Pleas** handled the bulk of Declaration of Intention ("first papers") and Petition for Naturalization ("second papers") filings. Originals at the Schuylkill County Courthouse; some on FamilySearch microfilm.
- **Content note:** 1840s–1860s naturalizations typically record name, country of allegiance renounced, date, and sometimes age/residence — but often do not specify birthplace below the national level.
- **Digitization level:** Passenger lists — indexed + images (free on FamilySearch, subscription on Ancestry). Naturalization records — some on FamilySearch microfilm; originals physical at courthouse.

### Tax records
- **Types:** County real estate tax, personal property tax, poll tax.
- **Date range:** County tax records begin 1811.
- **Value for 1840s–1860s:** Annual tax lists document property owners in each township with assessed valuations and sometimes occupation. Excellent intercensal tracking tool and substitute for the destroyed 1890 census.
- **Where held:** Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg (statewide microfilm); Schuylkill County Courthouse (original records); FamilySearch Catalog (check "Schuylkill, Pennsylvania, United States – Taxation").
- **Digitization level:** Microfilm to physical; no comprehensive online index for this county and period.
- **Note:** The Ancestry collection "Pennsylvania, U.S., Tax and Exoneration Lists, 1768–1801" covers only the pre-county-formation period (when this territory was in Berks/Northampton) and is not relevant to the 1840s–1860s.

### Occupational and mining records *(unique to this area)*
- **Types:** Company employment rolls, payroll records, mine inspector reports, accident reports. The Pennsylvania legislature appointed the first state mine inspector in 1869, generating official accident and inspection reports from that point.
- **Major employer:** Philadelphia and Reading Coal and Iron Company (the dominant enterprise by the 1860s) and its predecessors.
- **Where held:** Historical Society of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia (P&R corporate records); Schuylkill County Historical Society (local company records); Pennsylvania State Archives (mine inspection reports from 1870).
- **Digitization level:** Physical only; some finding aids available online.

### Other record types
- **Court of Common Pleas records:** Civil suits, criminal cases, equity proceedings. These often name family members, neighbors, and business associates, and can resolve identity questions when vital records are absent. Physical at Schuylkill County Courthouse.
- **School records:** Pennsylvania established a public school system in 1834. Township school records and county superintendent reports may be at the county courthouse, Pennsylvania State Archives, or local school district successor offices.
- **Poor relief records:** County commissioners administered poor relief for indigent miners and immigrant families. Records at the county courthouse.
- **Orphans' Court records:** Guardianship proceedings for minor children appear within the probate/court records at the courthouse and are especially useful for tracing families after a parent's death.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collections for this place | Access |
|---|---|---|
| FamilySearch | Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985 (1.2M records, indexed + images); US Federal Census 1840–1870 (indexed + images); Probate Records 1845–1880 (browse-only, fulltext-searchable); Lutheran Church Records 1840–1875 (browse-only images) | Free |
| Ancestry | 1850 US Federal Census – Schuylkill County (collection 1276, indexed + images); Civil War service/pension records; passenger lists; selected Pennsylvania records | Subscription |
| Fold3 | Civil War service records, pension files, Civil War draft (1863) | Subscription |
| FindAGrave | Cemetery transcriptions for Schuylkill County | Free |
| Newspapers.com / GenealogyBank | Partial Miners' Journal and Pennsylvania newspaper coverage | Subscription |
| Chronicling America (LOC) | Selected Pennsylvania newspapers; check for Schuylkill County titles | Free |
| FamilySearch Catalog | Finding aid for all microfilmed records not yet digitized (deeds, tax lists, naturalization, church records) | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| Schuylkill County Courthouse, Pottsville | Deed books (Recorder of Deeds); wills and probate files (Register of Wills); naturalizations and court records (Court of Common Pleas); marriages from 1885 | In-person or mail |
| Schuylkill County Historical Society, Pottsville | Local manuscript collections, cemetery files, Miners' Journal microfilm, church record microfilm, mining company records, local genealogies | In-person; contact for hours |
| Pottsville Free Public Library | Miners' Journal microfilm; local history reference | In-person |
| Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg | Tax records, adjutant general Civil War records, Penn's Land Office warrants and surveys | In-person; many records on FamilySearch microfilm |
| Historical Society of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia | P&R Coal and Iron Company records; statewide genealogical collections; newspapers | In-person; membership may be required |
| Diocese of Allentown Archives | Roman Catholic parish records for Schuylkill County | Contact by mail/email; in-person by appointment |
| Presbyterian Historical Society, Philadelphia | Presbyterian congregation records | In-person; some digitized |
| NARA Mid-Atlantic Region, Philadelphia | Federal court records, some naturalization records | In-person or mail; many digitized on Ancestry/Fold3 |
| NARA Washington DC / St. Louis | Civil War service records and pension files | Mail/online request; most on Fold3/Ancestry |

---

## Records NOT online

The following exist for this period but are **not** in any fully searchable online database:

- **Deed books (land records):** Some FamilySearch microfilm; originals at Recorder of Deeds. Not fully indexed online.
- **Tax records (1840s–1860s):** FamilySearch microfilm and State Archives; no comprehensive online index for this county/period.
- **Naturalization records:** Some FamilySearch microfilm; originals at the courthouse. Not comprehensively indexed.
- **Lutheran church records (browse-only):** Image group 004288771 on FamilySearch — 198 images, no name index, no fulltext search. Browse image by image.
- **Most non-Lutheran church records:** Welsh Calvinist Methodist, Catholic, Methodist, Episcopal, and Baptist congregation records are held by denominational archives or individual churches; many have not been microfilmed.
- **Probate records (most of the volume):** Image group 004812990 — 286 images, only 4% indexed; fulltext search helps but much of the volume still requires browsing.
- **Miners' Journal (bulk of the run):** Extensive microfilm at Pottsville Free Public Library; not fully digitized or fulltext-searchable.
- **Mining company employment records:** Physical only at Historical Society of Pennsylvania and Schuylkill County Historical Society.
- **County court records (civil/criminal dockets):** Physical at Schuylkill County Courthouse; not digitized.
- **School records and poor relief records:** Physical at county courthouse and Pennsylvania State Archives.

---

## Known record losses

- **No major courthouse fire** is on record for Schuylkill County. The Pottsville courthouse records are substantially intact from 1811 onward — a significant advantage compared to many Pennsylvania counties.
- **Pennsylvania civil registration gap (1840–1885/1906):** This is a structural gap, not a destruction loss. The government simply did not require registration. Church records and substitute sources must fill it entirely.
- **1890 federal census:** Destroyed in a 1921 Washington DC fire. The resulting gap (1880–1900) falls mostly outside this guide's period but is worth noting for researchers tracing families across decades.
- **Individual church record losses:** Some congregations have lost records to fire, flooding, or poor storage. The Schuylkill County Historical Society's files can identify which congregations have surviving records.

---

## Research tips

1. **Church records are mandatory, not optional.** There was no civil registration in the 1840s–1860s. Identify the family's likely denomination first — Welsh family → Calvinist Methodist chapel; Irish family → Catholic parish; German family → Lutheran or Reformed. Then find that congregation's records.

2. **Start with FamilySearch Collection 1401638.** "Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985" has 1.2 million indexed records and is free. It should be the first search for any baptism, marriage, or burial.

3. **The Miners' Journal is irreplaceable.** For a coal-mining county, this newspaper's obituary and marriage columns are the closest thing to a vital register for much of the population. Contact the Pottsville Free Public Library to access microfilm.

4. **Use the 1850 and 1860 mortality schedules.** These record deaths in the preceding year with cause, age, and birthplace — vital information unavailable anywhere else for this period.

5. **Naturalization records are a high priority for immigrant families.** With massive Irish and German immigration, the Court of Common Pleas naturalization files are essential. Search the FamilySearch Catalog under "Schuylkill, Pennsylvania, United States – Naturalization and Citizenship" for microfilm holdings.

6. **The 48th Pennsylvania Infantry has been extensively researched.** If your ancestor served in the Civil War from Schuylkill County, NARA pension files for 48th Pennsylvania veterans are especially rich and often include pre-war family information.

7. **Welsh records may be in Welsh.** Welsh Calvinist Methodist records are sometimes written entirely in Welsh. The National Library of Wales and Welsh genealogical societies can assist with translation and record location.

8. **Search Berks and Northampton counties for pre-1811 records.** If your family was in this area before Schuylkill County was formed in 1811, their records will be in one of those two parent counties.

9. **Probate records compensate for missing vital records.** Orphans' Court guardianship files, estate inventories naming heirs, and widows' dower claims establish family relationships that civil vital records would otherwise provide.

10. **Contact the Schuylkill County Historical Society before visiting.** Their local manuscript collections, cemetery files, and vertical files on local families extend well beyond what is available online. Confirm hours and access procedures in advance.

# Locality Guide: Alabama Region — Pre-Statehood (circa 1780–1819)

## Jurisdiction overview

The most important framing fact for this research: **Alabama did not exist as a jurisdiction in 1800.** The region was part of the **Mississippi Territory (1798–1817)**, then the **Alabama Territory (1817–1819)**, before statehood on 14 December 1819. Records follow those jurisdictions, not modern Alabama boundaries.

The Mobile Bay area had an even longer colonial history — French (1702–1763), British (1763–1780), Spanish (1780–1813) — each with its own archive. The interior of present-day Alabama was largely Creek and Cherokee territory until after the Creek War (1813–1814), so significant European-American settlement there dates only from about 1805–1816.

**Population:** Tiny — roughly 7,600 non-Native people in the entire Mississippi Territory in 1800, growing to ~9,000 in the Alabama portion by the 1810 federal census, then surging after the Creek War.

---

## Boundary changes (and why they matter for records)

| Period | Jurisdiction | Record custodian |
|---|---|---|
| 1702–1763 | French colony | Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM), Aix-en-Provence |
| 1763–1780 | British West Florida | The National Archives (Kew, UK), CO 5 series |
| 1780–1813 | Spanish West Florida (Mobile District) | Archivo General de Indias, Seville; copies at Library of Congress |
| 1798–1817 | Mississippi Territory | ADAH (Montgomery); MDAH (Jackson, MS) |
| 1813 | U.S. seizes Mobile | U.S. federal and county records begin |
| 1817–1819 | Alabama Territory | ADAH; county courthouses |
| 1819– | State of Alabama | County courthouses; ADAH |

**County formation is critical.** Washington County (formed 1800) was the first county in present-day Alabama. Madison County followed in 1808, Baldwin County in 1809, and Montgomery County in 1816. Events before a county existed are recorded — if at all — at the territorial level or in a parent county.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)
- **Start date:** Alabama did not begin **statewide** civil registration until **1908**. No civil birth or death certificates exist for 1800.
- **Marriages:** County marriage bonds begin with each county's formation (Washington County, ca. 1800). **FamilySearch collection Alabama, County Marriages, 1809–1950** (ID 1598408) — 612,345 indexed records, 306,172 images — is the earliest accessible source. **Access: Indexed + images, free.**
- **Pre-registration substitute:** Church registers are the only birth/death source for this period.

### Church records
- **Roman Catholic** (dominant in Mobile since 1702): sacramental registers covering baptisms, marriages, and burials. Originals at the **Diocese of Mobile Archives**; French- and Spanish-era microfilm at the **Mobile Municipal Archives** and **Library of Congress**.
- **Methodist and Baptist** congregations organized ca. 1808–1812; early records are fragmentary, held locally and at the **Alabama Baptist Historical Commission** (Samford University, Birmingham).
- **FamilySearch collection Alabama, Church Records, 1803–1950** (ID 1986396) — 156,789 indexed records, 78,394 images. **Access: Indexed + images, free.**
- Original Catholic registers: **physical only** at Diocese of Mobile Archives; French/Spanish era: **microfilm** at Mobile Municipal Archives / Library of Congress.

### Census records
- **Federal census:** The **1810 census** is the first to cover Alabama (as Mississippi Territory). The 1800 federal census did not reach this area. **Access: Indexed + images** on FamilySearch and Ancestry.
- **Territorial censuses (critical for 1800–1809):** The Mississippi Territory conducted special enumerations in **1801, 1805, 1808, and 1810** covering present-day Alabama settlers. These are held at **ADAH** on microfilm. **Access: physical/microfilm at ADAH.** Some transcribed in published Alabama Genealogical Society volumes.
- **Compiled index:** FamilySearch collection Alabama, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes Index, 1810–1890 (ID 2016756) — 287,634 entries, **index only, no images**. Originals must be consulted at ADAH.

### Probate and court records
- Begin with each county's formation; Washington County records from ca. 1800.
- **FamilySearch collection Alabama, Probate Records, 1809–1985** (ID 1537764) — 438,210 indexed records, 219,105 images. **Access: Indexed + images, free.**
- Mississippi Territory Superior Court records (1799–1817) are at **ADAH** and **MDAH** (Jackson, MS). **Access: physical/microfilm.**
- Original courthouse volumes: **physical only** at county probate judge's office.

### Land records
- Alabama is a **federal land state** — most land was distributed via the U.S. General Land Office, not by the state.
- **BLM General Land Office Records** (glorecords.blm.gov): all federal land patents including earliest territorial grants from ca. 1803. **Access: Indexed + images, free.**
- **Spanish and British grants (Mobile area):** Private land claim files at **NARA** (Record Group 49); original Spanish grants at **Archivo General de Indias** (Seville). **Access: physical/microfilm only.**
- County deed books: at county courthouses and on ADAH microfilm; **not digitized online** for the pre-1820 period.

### Military records
- **War of 1812 / Creek War (1813–1814)** is the primary military event. Compiled Military Service Records and pension files at **NARA** (Record Group 94). Pension files often contain detailed family depositions.
- **Fold3** and **Ancestry** have indexed War of 1812 service and pension records. **Access: Indexed + images, subscription.**
- **Bounty land warrants:** at NARA and BLM GLO.
- **Territorial militia rolls:** at ADAH on microfilm. **Access: physical/microfilm.**
- **Revolutionary War pensions:** settlers who moved from other states may have NARA pension files noting Alabama residence — check Fold3 and Ancestry.

### Tax records
- Territorial and early county tax digests (property and poll tax) are the **single best census substitute for the pre-1810 period**. They name heads of household annually.
- Earliest: Washington County tax records from ca. 1809. Other counties from their formation dates.
- Held at **ADAH** (microfilm); originals at county courthouses. Some transcribed in genealogical society publications.
- **Access: physical/microfilm at ADAH.**

### Colonial records (Mobile area, pre-1798)

| Period | Repository | Access |
|---|---|---|
| French (1702–1763) | ANOM, Aix-en-Provence; microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives | Physical / microfilm |
| British (1763–1780) | The National Archives, Kew (CO 5 series) | Physical |
| Spanish (1780–1813) | Archivo General de Indias, Seville (*Papeles Procedentes de Cuba*); microfilm at Library of Congress and Mobile Municipal Archives | Physical / microfilm |

Spanish records include *padrones* (census returns), sacramental registers, land grants, and military rosters — rich sources if your family was in the Mobile District.

### Cemetery records
- No systematic records exist for this period. Family plots and early church graveyards predominate.
- **FindAGrave** (findagrave.com) and **BillionGraves** (billiongraves.com) have user-contributed transcriptions, but pre-1820 rural coverage is thin. **Access: free online.**
- Published county cemetery surveys at ADAH and genealogical society libraries are often more complete.

### Newspapers
- First Alabama newspaper: *Mobile Centinel* (1811). *Huntsville Republican* ca. 1816.
- Pre-1811, consult Natchez, MS newspapers (Mississippi Territory capital).
- **Chronicling America** (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) has limited pre-1820 Alabama coverage. **Access: free.**
- ADAH holds early Alabama newspaper microfilm.

---

## Records NOT online

The following records **exist** but are not in any major online database for this period:

- Mississippi Territory census substitutes (1801, 1805, 1808) — ADAH microfilm only
- County deed books pre-1820 — courthouse originals / ADAH microfilm
- Washington County records pre-1809 — predate the FamilySearch collection start date
- Spanish colonial *padrones* and land grant files — overseas archives / Library of Congress microfilm
- French colonial administrative and church records — ANOM or Mobile Municipal Archives microfilm
- British West Florida records — The National Archives, Kew
- Early territorial militia rolls — ADAH microfilm
- NARA private land claim files (Spanish/British confirmed grants, Record Group 49)
- Catholic sacramental register originals — Diocese of Mobile Archives

---

## Known record losses and gaps

- **Frontier record-keeping was uneven throughout.** Washington County and the earliest territorial courts operated with limited staff and resources; gaps in all record types before 1810 should be expected.
- **Creek War disruption (1813–1814):** Military operations across central and southern Alabama disrupted county administration.
- **Washington County:** Some early county records were damaged or lost before systematic preservation efforts began; surviving records are microfilmed at ADAH.
- **No civil vital records at all before 1908.** Church registers and county marriage bonds are the only substitute.

**Substitute strategies when records are missing:**
- BLM GLO federal patents independently document land ownership even when deed books are lost
- NARA War of 1812 pension depositions frequently reconstruct family relationships in detail
- *Territorial Papers of the United States*, Mississippi Territory series (NARA microfilm M764; also published volumes edited by Clarence Carter) contain petitions, name-lists, and land claims
- Tax digests provide annual name-lists standing in for missing census years

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Key holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | County marriages 1809– (612K, images); probate 1809– (438K, images); church records 1803– (157K, images); compiled census substitutes index 1810– (288K, index only) | Free |
| **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov) | All federal land patents for Alabama, ca. 1803 onward | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | 1810 and 1820 federal census; War of 1812 pension/service indexes | Subscription |
| **Fold3** (fold3.com) | War of 1812 service records, pension files; Revolutionary War pensions | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** (loc.gov/chroniclingamerica) | Early Alabama newspapers (limited pre-1820) | Free |
| **FindAGrave** (findagrave.com) | Cemetery transcriptions (thin pre-1820 coverage) | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Alabama Dept. of Archives and History (ADAH)**, 624 Washington Ave., Montgomery, AL 36130 | Territorial census substitutes; tax digests; county record microfilm; militia rolls; newspapers | In-person; some records at archives.alabama.gov |
| **NARA**, Washington, D.C. | War of 1812 service/pension records (RG 94); private land claims for Mobile (RG 49); Territorial Papers M764 | In-person; many records on Fold3/Ancestry |
| **County courthouses** (probate judge offices) | Original deed books, will books, marriage bonds, court minutes | In-person |
| **Mobile Municipal Archives**, 457 Church St., Mobile, AL | French- and Spanish-period records on microfilm; early Mobile municipal records | In-person; appointment recommended |
| **Diocese of Mobile Archives**, 400 Government St., Mobile, AL | Original Catholic sacramental registers, French colonial period forward | In-person; written inquiry recommended |
| **Mississippi Dept. of Archives and History (MDAH)**, Jackson, MS | Mississippi Territory Superior Court records (1799–1817); Natchez-district records | In-person; some records at mdah.ms.gov |
| **Library of Congress** (Manuscript Div.), Washington, D.C. | Microfilm of Spanish *Papeles Procedentes de Cuba*; some French colonial materials | In-person; some digitized at loc.gov |
| **Samford Univ. / Alabama Baptist Historical Commission**, Birmingham, AL | Early Baptist church records and minutes | In-person |
| **Tulane University Special Collections**, New Orleans, LA | Louisiana and West Florida Spanish colonial records | In-person |
| **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)**, Aix-en-Provence, France | French colonial administrative and church records for Mobile (1702–1763) | In-person or written request |
| **Archivo General de Indias**, Seville, Spain | Spanish colonial records for the Mobile District, *padrones*, land grants (1780–1813) | In-person; some docs at pares.mcu.es |

---

## Research tips

1. **Start at ADAH before anything else.** It is the primary repository for territorial-era Alabama. Their online catalog lists all microfilm holdings, and staff can answer repository questions: (334) 242-4435 / archives.alabama.gov.

2. **Alabama is a federal land state — begin at BLM GLO.** The free BLM site (glorecords.blm.gov) is fully indexed and gives you the legal land description (township/range/section) that anchors your ancestor to a precise location and identifies the correct early county.

3. **Follow the jurisdiction backward, not the modern state forward.** Events in 1800 are Mississippi Territory events. Events in Mobile in 1800 are Spanish colonial events. Each political transition produced a new record custodian and a new archive.

4. **Mine War of 1812 pension files aggressively.** These are some of the richest narrative sources in 19th-century American genealogy. A pension application can run dozens of pages, with sworn depositions from neighbors naming parents, siblings, birthdates, and prior residences. Search Fold3 first, then cross-check NARA.

5. **Read the Territorial Papers of the United States, Mississippi Territory series.** Published volumes (ed. Clarence Carter) and NARA microfilm M764 contain petitions, land-claim depositions, and correspondence that name individual settlers — an underused source for this period.

6. **Distinguish Mobile-area families from interior settlers.** Mobile families have Catholic records back to 1702 and Spanish records from 1780. Interior (Madison, Washington, Tuscaloosa counties) families are almost all post-1800 migrants from the Carolinas, Virginia, Georgia, or Tennessee — their pre-Alabama records will be in those origin states.

7. **For Spanish-era Mobile, consider the Papeles Procedentes de Cuba.** This series at the Archivo General de Indias covers Spanish West Florida administration. Microfilm copies are at the Library of Congress. The FamilySearch Wiki article "Alabama Genealogy" (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Alabama_Genealogy) — specifically the Colonial Records and Territorial Records sections — provides the best single online orientation to these sources.

8. **If your ancestor may have been Native American or intermarried with Native families**, NARA Record Group 75 (Bureau of Indian Affairs) covers Creek and Cherokee records for this period, including treaty records, removal-era census rolls, and annuity payment lists.

---

# Locality Guide: Arkansas Region — Spanish Colonial Period (1763–1800)

## Critical Framing

Before diving into records, one jurisdictional fact shapes everything: **"Arkansas" did not exist** as a named American jurisdiction until 1819 (Territory) or 1836 (State). Before 1803, the region was part of French and then Spanish *Louisiana*. All pre-1800 European settlement centered on a single place: **Arkansas Post** (*Poste des Arkansas / Puesto de Arkansas*), a garrison and trading post at the confluence of the Arkansas and Mississippi rivers. That is where nearly all surviving records were created.

The succession of controlling powers was:
- **French Louisiana** (La Louisiane) — to 1762/63
- **Spanish Louisiana** (Luisiana) — Treaty of Fontainebleau 1762, confirmed Treaty of Paris 1763, until the secret Treaty of San Ildefonso in **1800**
- French (nominal, 1800–1803) → **Louisiana Purchase**, 1803 → U.S. territory
- Arkansas Territory created **1819**; statehood **1836**

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

| Factor | Detail |
|---|---|
| Administrative center | Arkansas Post — sole significant European settlement |
| Superior authority | Governor-General of Louisiana, New Orleans; Louisiana was administered via the Captaincy-General of **Cuba** |
| Population (ca. 1791 padrón) | ~400–500 European and mixed-ancestry residents at the Post |
| Economy | Fur trade, military garrison, subsistence agriculture |
| Ethnic composition | French Creole civilians (majority), Spanish officers/officials, Anglo-American traders, Quapaw and other Native peoples |
| Religion | Roman Catholic exclusively |

---

## Available Record Types

### ⛪ Church Records (Sacramental Registers)
**These are the functional equivalent of vital records for this period.**

Catholic missionaries (Recollect Franciscans, then Capuchins) maintained registers of baptisms, marriages, and burials at Arkansas Post from as early as the 1720s through the Spanish period. Entries name individuals, parents, godparents, and witnesses, making them excellent for family reconstruction. Native American converts (primarily Quapaw) also appear.

| Repository | Holdings | Digitization Level |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** — Image Group **008814402** | 64 images, Sacramental Registers (Spanish Colonial), Arkansas Post, **1763–1800**, in Spanish | ⚠️ **Browse-only** — 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable; must be viewed image by image |
| **Roman Catholic Diocese of Little Rock** | Original or custody of surviving registers | Physical only |
| **Arkansas History Commission**, Little Rock | Transcribed/abstracted copies | In-person or written inquiry |

> **Practical note:** 64 images is a manageable browse. Each sacramental entry typically names several people (parents, godparents, witnesses), so yield per image is high.

---

### 📋 Padrones (Colonial Censuses)
Under Spain, governors periodically ordered *padrones* — population counts listing head of household, family members with ages, origin, occupation, and sometimes property. They are among the most genealogically valuable pre-statehood sources for this region.

**Known padrones for the Arkansas district:** ca. **1791** (~400+ residents) and **1798**, plus periodic interim counts. No U.S. federal census covered this territory until 1810 (Missouri Territory).

| Repository | Access |
|---|---|
| **Archivo General de Indias (AGI)**, Seville — *Papeles Procedentes de Cuba* section | Physical (Seville) or via **PARES** (pares.mcu.es) — free Spanish archive portal |
| **Library of Congress**, Manuscript Division | Microfilm of Spanish colonial Louisiana records |
| **Arkansas History Commission** | Published transcriptions and abstracts |

**Digitization level:** Microfilm (Library of Congress, partial) or physical (AGI Seville). Not indexed on any major genealogy platform.

---

### 🏡 Land Records (Concesiones de Tierras)
Spain issued land concessions naming the grantee, family status, land description, witnesses, and date. **A critical backdoor:** when Spanish-era claimants filed for U.S. confirmation of their grants after 1803, they had to document the original grant in detail. These U.S.-era files often contain rich family information.

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **AGI, Seville** — *Papeles de Cuba* | Original Spanish grants | Physical / PARES |
| **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov) | U.S. post-1803 *confirmations* of Spanish/French grants | ✅ **Indexed + images online** (free) |
| **NARA** — RG 49 | U.S. land claim confirmation files describing original colonial grants | Physical; some microfilm |

---

### ⚔️ Military Records
Arkansas Post was primarily a garrison — typically 20–50 Spanish soldiers plus officers. Military records include muster rolls (*revistas de comisario*), officer service records, and garrison correspondence to the Governor in New Orleans.

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **AGI, Seville** — *Papeles de Cuba* and *Santo Domingo* | Muster rolls, officer records, commandant correspondence | Physical / PARES |
| **Library of Congress** | Microfilm of Spanish colonial Louisiana military records | On-site microfilm |
| **Archivo General de Simancas** (Spain) | Spanish military officer service records | Physical / PARES |

---

### 📜 Civil Vital Records
**Did not exist.** Civil registration was not established in this region during the colonial period. The Catholic sacramental registers are the only equivalent. Arkansas statewide civil registration did not begin until **1914**.

---

### ⚖️ Probate and Court Records
The Post commandant held civil and judicial authority. Surviving records are part of the administrative correspondence in the *Papeles de Cuba* at the AGI. American-style county probate records did not begin until **Arkansas County was created in 1813**.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online

| Repository | What's Relevant | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch.org** | 1 browse-only volume: Arkansas Post Sacramental Registers 1763–1800 (Image Group 008814402, 64 images, Spanish); 3 indexed collections exist for Arkansas but all begin **1819 or later** — no indexed colonial content | Free |
| **PARES** (pares.mcu.es) | Finding aids and some digitized images from the AGI, including the *Papeles de Cuba* | Free |
| **BLM GLO** (glorecords.blm.gov) | U.S. confirmations of Spanish/French land grants | Free |

### Physical / Microfilm

| Repository | Key Holdings |
|---|---|
| **Arkansas History Commission**, Little Rock, AR | Primary state repository; transcriptions of padrones, church register abstracts, published colonial guides; ark-ives.com for finding aids |
| **Archivo General de Indias (AGI)**, Seville, Spain — *Papeles de Cuba* | **The most comprehensive repository** for Spanish Louisiana records: padrones, land grants, military records, commandant correspondence |
| **Library of Congress**, Manuscript Division | Microfilm of Spanish colonial Louisiana records, including portions of the *Papeles de Cuba* |
| **Roman Catholic Diocese of Little Rock** | Original/custody of surviving sacramental registers |
| **Historic New Orleans Collection** & **Louisiana State Archives**, New Orleans/Baton Rouge | Louisiana colonial and Spanish-period records; Arkansas Post residents appear in New Orleans notarial and court records |
| **Arkansas Post National Memorial**, Gillett, AR (NPS) | Interpretive and bibliographic resources; staff referrals to specialists |

---

## Records NOT Online (But They Exist)

- **Padrones (colonial censuses)** — 1791, 1798, others — not indexed on any genealogy platform
- **Original Spanish land concessions** — AGI Seville; only U.S. post-1803 confirmations are online
- **Military muster rolls and garrison records** — AGI Seville; some Library of Congress microfilm, not name-indexed online
- **Administrative correspondence** — commandant letters to New Orleans governor; at AGI; name many individuals; not online
- **Full sacramental register holdings** — what's digitized on FamilySearch (64 images) is only partial, and is browse-only

---

## Known Record Losses

- **Frontier irregularity:** Record-keeping at a small remote garrison was inconsistent; some years have no surviving registers at all.
- **Records retained by departing colonial powers:** When Spain and then France relinquished Louisiana, some records traveled with the administration to Spanish and French archives rather than staying locally.
- **French-period records:** Pre-1763 materials are in French colonial archives (*Archives nationales d'outre-mer*, Aix-en-Provence, France), not typically accessible through American repositories.
- **Physical losses:** Repeated flooding at the Arkansas Post site damaged records over time.
- **Extremely sparse population:** Many individuals who passed through the Arkansas district left no traceable record at all.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with the Arkansas History Commission** before making plans to travel to Seville. Their staff holds transcriptions, finding aids, and published guides for Arkansas colonial research and can focus your search significantly.

2. **PARES is free.** The Spanish government's portal (pares.mcu.es) provides finding aids and some digitized documents from the AGI's *Papeles de Cuba*. Search for "Arkansas," "Posta de Arkansas," or "Puesto de Arkansas."

3. **The Library of Congress microfilm collection is underused.** LOC holds a significant microfilm set of Spanish colonial Louisiana records (*Papeles de Cuba* portions). Accessible in Washington without traveling to Spain, and not widely known among U.S. genealogists.

4. **Published transcriptions exist.** Genealogists have transcribed and translated key padrones and church records. Check the *Arkansas Historical Quarterly*, the *National Genealogical Society Quarterly*, and specialist works on Louisiana colonial records (Winston De Ville's publications are a starting point).

5. **Look sideways into Louisiana records.** Because Arkansas Post was administratively under New Orleans, significant records about its residents appear in New Orleans colonial materials — notarial records, court files, church records at the Historic New Orleans Collection and the New Orleans Notarial Archives.

6. **U.S. land claim files are a backdoor into colonial genealogy.** When Spanish-era grantees filed for U.S. confirmation post-1803, their files (NARA RG 49; BLM GLO online) often describe the original grantee's family in detail. This is frequently the most *accessible* path to the underlying colonial-era grant.

7. **Translation is essential — and must be precise.** Post-1769 records are in Spanish; pre-1769 in French; church formulas in Latin throughout. Colonial paleography (the handwriting style) adds another layer of difficulty. Do not rely on automated translation for genealogical records.

8. **Consider DNA alongside documentary research.** Given extremely sparse surviving records, autosomal and Y-DNA testing may connect you with descendants of the same small Arkansas Post community who hold additional documentary evidence.

---

# Locality Guide: Ohio / Northwest Territory (c. 1787–1803)

> **Critical jurisdictional note:** Ohio did not exist as a state in 1790. The entire region was governed as the **Northwest Territory**, organized by the Northwest Ordinance of 1787. At the county level, only **Washington County** (formed 1788, seat: Marietta) and **Hamilton County** (formed 1790, seat: Cincinnati) existed during your target period. Every search must begin in one of these two counties — not in any modern Ohio county.

---

## Jurisdiction overview

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| Governing authority | Northwest Territory (U.S. Congress, Northwest Ordinance of 1787) |
| Territorial capital | Marietta (1788–1800), then Chillicothe (1800–1803) |
| Ohio statehood | March 1, 1803 — first state from the Northwest Territory |
| Counties in 1790 | Washington County (1788) and Hamilton County (1790) only |
| Population c. 1790 | ~3,000–5,000 Euro-American settlers, concentrated along the Ohio River |
| Economy | Subsistence farming, fur trade, river commerce, land surveying/speculation |
| Settlers' origins | Primarily Virginia, Pennsylvania, and New England (Connecticut) |
| Native nations | Shawnee, Delaware, Wyandot, Miami — active throughout the interior |
| Denominations | Presbyterian, Methodist, Baptist, Quaker, Moravian, Catholic (French settlers) |

---

## Boundary changes — the most important section for 1790 research

Records follow the jurisdiction that created them, not modern lines. The table below shows what existed when:

| Date | Event | What it means for you |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-1788 | No organized Ohio county government | No county records exist; look to federal/territorial authorities |
| July 27, 1788 | **Washington County** organized, covering nearly all present-day Ohio east of the Scioto | Earliest Marietta-area records start here |
| January 2, 1790 | **Hamilton County** organized from western Washington County | Cincinnati / southwestern Ohio records shift here |
| 1795–1803 | Rapid county formation after Treaty of Greenville — Adams, Clermont, Fairfield, Jefferson, Ross, Trumbull, and others carved out | Each new county pulled records from its parent — always verify the correct county for an event date |
| March 1, 1803 | Ohio statehood | Territorial record-keeping transitions to state authority |

**Rule of thumb:** Southeastern Ohio (Marietta, Ohio Company purchase) → Washington County. Southwestern Ohio (Cincinnati, Symmes Purchase) → Hamilton County. Northeastern Ohio (Western Reserve) → not yet organized as Ohio counties in 1790 — these were claimed by Connecticut and settlement was sparse until after 1795.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)
- **Start date:** **No statewide civil registration during the territorial period.** Ohio did not require statewide birth and death registration until **1867**.
- **Marriages:** County-level marriage registration began in the territorial era. FamilySearch Collection **"Ohio, County Marriages, 1789–2013"** (ID 1803785) covers this period — **1,523,678 indexed records**, 3,047,356 persons, 761,839 images. → **Indexed + images.**
- **Births/Deaths:** No civil registration in 1790. Church records and family Bibles are the only substitutes.
- **Gap:** Do not expect a birth or death register for any Ohio location before 1867. This is not a record loss — it is a record-type that simply did not exist.

### Church records
- **Denominations present in 1790:** Moravian missions (pre-date American settlement — Schoenbrunn, 1772); Methodist circuit riders, Presbyterian missionaries, and Baptist congregations followed settlers after 1788.
- **FamilySearch Collection:** **"Ohio, Church Records, 1787–1950"** (ID 2246917) — **678,234 indexed records**, 339,117 images. → **Indexed + images.**
- **Additional holdings:** Ohio History Connection (Columbus); Western Reserve Historical Society (Cleveland, for Reserve-area records); Moravian Archives (Bethlehem, PA for mission records).
- **Language:** Predominantly English; Moravian records may be in German.
- **Coverage warning:** Very few congregations were organized in 1790. Surviving registers from 1788–1795 are sparse — don't assume a record exists simply because a congregation was present.

### Census records
- **1790 federal census:** The Northwest Territory was **NOT enumerated.** This is one of the most significant documentation gaps for this period. Ohio settlers in 1790 appear in no federal census.
- **1800 federal census:** The Northwest Territory was enumerated, but some schedules are incomplete or lost. Ohio appears as a state beginning in the **1810 census**.
- **Substitute:** **Territorial and county tax lists are the primary substitute for a census in 1790** — they list heads of household with taxable property year by year.

### Probate and court records
- **Begins:** Washington County, **1788**; Hamilton County, **1790**
- **Where held:** Original records at county courthouses (Washington County Courthouse, Marietta; Hamilton County Courthouse, Cincinnati). Major territorial-era holdings also at the **Ohio History Connection** (OHC), Columbus.
- **Digitization level:** Not found in any FamilySearch indexed collection for the 1788–1803 window. Check FamilySearch Catalog by county name for microfilm. Likely **microfilm** or **physical only**.
- **Research value:** Probate records frequently name siblings, parents, neighbors as bondsmen, and creditors — invaluable for family reconstruction when vital records are absent.

### Land records — the most complex category; requires knowing your district

The territory was divided into **distinct land-grant districts**, each with a different survey system and repository. Determine your ancestor's district first.

| District | Location | Survey system | Key repository |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Seven Ranges** | Eastern Ohio | Rectangular (1st federal survey, 1786) | BLM GLO website; NARA |
| **Ohio Company Purchase** | Around Marietta / Washington County | Rectangular | OHC; BLM GLO |
| **Symmes (Miami) Purchase** | SW Ohio, Hamilton County | Rectangular | BLM GLO; Hamilton County |
| **Virginia Military District** | Between Scioto and Little Miami rivers | **Metes and bounds — no township/range** | Virginia State Library (Richmond); OHC |
| **Connecticut Western Reserve** | Northeastern Ohio | Rectangular | Western Reserve Historical Society; CT State Library |
| **U.S. Military District** | North-central Ohio | Rectangular | BLM GLO; NARA |

- **FamilySearch Collection:** **"Ohio, Land Records, 1787–1840"** (ID 1542187) — **234,567 indexed records**, 117,283 images. → **Indexed + images.**
- **Browse-only volume:** FamilySearch image group **005112233** — Northwest Territory Territorial Land Records, 1788–1799, **51 images, 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable.** Access is browse-only, image by image.
- **BLM GLO website** (glorecords.blm.gov): Federal land patents freely searchable online. Virginia Military District warrants require the Virginia State Library.
- **Critical distinction:** Virginia Military District uses metes-and-bounds descriptions referencing natural landmarks — fundamentally different from township/range descriptions elsewhere in Ohio.

### Military records
- **Relevant conflicts:** Northwest Indian Wars (1785–1795): Harmar's campaign (1790), St. Clair's defeat (1791), Battle of Fallen Timbers (1794). Many early settlers were also Revolutionary War veterans.
- **Record types:** Revolutionary War pension files and bounty land warrants; Northwest Indian Wars service records; Virginia Military District warrant files; territorial militia musters (fragmentary).
- **Where held:** NARA and Fold3 (fold3.com) for federal records. Ohio History Connection for territorial militia. Virginia State Library for VMD warrants.
- **Digitization level:** Revolutionary War pension files — **Indexed + images** on Fold3 and Ancestry. Territorial militia — **physical only** at OHC.

### Tax records
- **Date range:** Washington County tax lists survive from **1788**; Hamilton County from **1790**.
- **FamilySearch Collection:** **"Ohio, Tax Records, 1800–1850"** (ID 1987542) — **345,890 indexed records**, 172,945 images. Starts in 1800; does not cover 1788–1799. → **Indexed + images.**
- **Browse-only volume:** FamilySearch image group **005112240** — Washington County Tax Records, 1799–1803 — **73 images, 12% name-indexed**, not full-text searchable. The remaining 88% must be browsed image by image.
- **Pre-1800 tax records:** Likely **physical only** at Washington County Courthouse and OHC. Check FamilySearch Catalog for any surviving microfilm.

### Cemetery records
- **Coverage in 1790:** Very few formal cemeteries existed. Most burials were in family plots. Church cemeteries formed alongside congregations post-1788.
- **Online:** FindAGrave (curated FamilySearch external resource) covers many Ohio cemeteries including early Washington and Hamilton County sites. 1790-era stones that survive are rare and often weathered.
- **DAR transcriptions:** The Daughters of the American Revolution conducted extensive Ohio cemetery surveys in the early 20th century; transcriptions held at the OHC.

### Newspaper records
- **Earliest papers:** *Centinel of the North-Western Territory* (Cincinnati, 1793) — first newspaper in the Northwest Territory. *Ohio Gazette* (Marietta, 1801). Coverage in 1790 is essentially nonexistent.
- **Where held:** Newspapers.com (curated external resource). Ohio History Connection (microfilm). Chronicling America (loc.gov/chroniclingamerica — free).
- **Value:** Even sparse territorial papers carried legal notices (estate settlements, land sales) that may name your ancestor.

### Immigration and naturalization
- **Migration routes:** Most 1790 settlers arrived via the Ohio River from Pittsburgh (Forbes Road / Braddock's Road from the East) or overland from Virginia and Kentucky. No Atlantic ports are directly relevant to 1790 Ohio research.
- **Trace origins using:** Pennsylvania and Virginia land records; Revolutionary War pension files (often give birthplace and migration narrative in the applicant's own words); Atlantic passenger lists for foreign-born settlers who arrived earlier.
- **Naturalization:** Territorial courts could naturalize; records at county courthouses and OHC. Very few documented for 1787–1803.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | What's available | Access |
|---|---|---|
| FamilySearch (familysearch.org) | County Marriages 1789–2013; Land Records 1787–1840; Church Records 1787–1950; Tax Records 1800–1850; 2 browse-only territorial volumes | Free |
| BLM General Land Office (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents for all Ohio survey districts except Virginia Military District | Free |
| Fold3 (fold3.com) | Revolutionary War pensions; Northwest Indian Wars service records | Subscription |
| Ancestry (ancestry.com) | U.S. Federal Census 1800+; Revolutionary War records | Subscription |
| Chronicling America (loc.gov) | Early Ohio newspapers (sparse pre-1803) | Free |
| FindAGrave (findagrave.com) | Ohio cemetery transcriptions | Free |
| Newspapers.com | Historical Ohio newspapers (minimal 1790-era coverage) | Subscription |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Ohio History Connection (OHC)**, Columbus OH | Territorial court/government records; early county records; DAR cemetery transcriptions; WPA record copies; newspaper microfilm | In-person; ohiohistory.org |
| **Washington County Courthouse**, Marietta OH | Original deeds, probate, court records from 1788 | In-person or mail |
| **Hamilton County Courthouse**, Cincinnati OH | Original deeds, probate, court records from 1790 | In-person or mail |
| **Western Reserve Historical Society**, Cleveland OH | Connecticut Western Reserve land records; NE Ohio genealogy | In-person; library.wrhs.org |
| **Virginia State Library & Archives**, Richmond VA | Virginia Military District warrant files and survey plats | In-person; lva.virginia.gov |
| **Moravian Archives**, Bethlehem PA | Mission registers from Schoenbrunn, Gnadenhütten, other Ohio stations | By appointment; moravianchurcharchives.org |
| **NARA**, Washington D.C. | Military pension files; NWT Territorial Papers; 1800 census (NWT schedules) | In-person; many records on Fold3/Ancestry |
| **Ohio Auditor of State / State Archives**, Columbus OH | Survey plats and field notes | In-person |
| **Connecticut State Library**, Hartford CT | Connecticut Western Reserve original documents | In-person; some digital |

---

## Records NOT online

These record types are known to exist but are **not available in any name-indexed online database**:

- Territorial government executive and legislative records (physical — OHC and NARA)
- Washington County probate records, 1788–1800 (microfilm only — check FamilySearch Catalog)
- Hamilton County probate records, 1790–1800 (microfilm only — check FamilySearch Catalog)
- Territorial militia muster rolls (fragmentary — physical at OHC)
- Washington County tax lists, 1788–1799 (physical at courthouse and OHC; pre-1800 not in the indexed collection)
- Early church registers, 1788–1800 (mostly at denominational archives or OHC, not yet indexed)
- Virginia Military District survey plats and warrant files (physical at Virginia State Library)
- Moravian mission registers (microfilmed but not name-indexed online)
- Surveyor field notes and plats (physical at Ohio Auditor of State and NARA)

---

## Known record losses and gaps

| Loss | Details | Substitutes |
|---|---|---|
| **1790 federal census: Ohio absent entirely** | Northwest Territory not enumerated | Territorial and county tax lists, 1788–1799 |
| **Gnadenhütten Moravian mission records** | Mission community destroyed in 1782 massacre; records fragmentary | Surviving Moravian Archives records in Bethlehem, PA |
| **Territorial militia records** | No systematic preservation; only fragments survive | NARA federal military records; pension files |
| **Most pre-1795 church registers** | Frontier congregations kept informal records; many lost | Family Bibles; newspaper notices; probate records naming heirs |
| **Partial 1800 NWT census schedules** | Some Northwest Territory schedules incomplete or lost | County tax lists; territorial government records |

---

## Research tips

1. **Determine the land district before anything else.** Your ancestor's Virginia Military District vs. Ohio Company Purchase vs. Seven Ranges experience determines entirely which repository holds the records. Identify the district from the land description before choosing a repository.

2. **Use tax lists as your 1790 "census."** Washington County lists survive from 1788, Hamilton County from 1790. Browse-only FamilySearch volume 005112240 covers 1799–1803 (73 images, 12% indexed). Pre-1799 records require in-person or mail contact with the OHC or courthouse.

3. **Trace origins before Ohio.** Most 1790 settlers came from Virginia, Pennsylvania, New Jersey, or New England. Work backward into the origin state when Ohio records are sparse. A Revolutionary War pension file often provides birthplace, age, and a migration narrative in the applicant's own words.

4. **County formation was rapid.** After 1795, new counties were created quickly. A person in a given location may appear in Washington County records in 1792 but in a new county by 1797. Always verify which county held jurisdiction at the event date — the FamilySearch Wiki article "Ohio Genealogy" (familysearch.org/en/wiki/Ohio_Genealogy#Northwest_Territory) lists county formation dates.

5. **WPA transcription projects.** In the 1930s–1940s, the Works Progress Administration typed up many early Ohio court, deed, and vital records. These transcriptions — often more legible than the originals — are held at the OHC and some county libraries, and can bridge access gaps.

6. **FamilySearch Catalog by place.** For any specific county, search familysearch.org/catalog by county name to find all microfilmed and digitized holdings. Many pre-1803 records were microfilmed by the Genealogical Society of Utah and available at Family History Centers even when not indexed online.

7. **Virginia Military District ancestors:** Records are split — begin at the Virginia State Library for the warrant file, then look for the Ohio survey and deed at the county courthouse or OHC.

8. **FAN club research.** In a frontier community of 1790, neighbors, document witnesses, and bondsmen were frequently kin or former neighbors from the origin state. Researching the whole community often breaks through documentation walls that a person-only search cannot.

---

*Sources: FamilySearch Wiki, "Ohio Genealogy" (familysearch.org/en/wiki/Ohio_Genealogy); Wikipedia, "Ohio"; FamilySearch collections search and volume search results; external links survey. Guide written 2026-06-22.*
