# Locality Guide: Port of Boston — Immigration Records (1840s)

## Jurisdiction overview

Boston, Suffolk County, Massachusetts was one of the five principal U.S. ports of entry in the 1840s. The decade was defined by the Great Famine immigration wave: by 1850 more than a quarter of Boston's ~137,000 residents were Irish-born. Suffolk County's records survived intact — no courthouse disaster — but the period's mass mortality (cholera, 1849; typhus, 1847–1849) created gaps in official registration.

---

## The core immigration record: Customs Passenger Lists, 1820–1891

**Authority:** U.S. Customs Service, Port of Boston — NARA Record Group 36

The 1840s manifests were filed under the **Steerage Act of 1819** and record only **name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin**. Unlike post-1895 Ellis Island manifests, they do *not* record hometown, last residence, or destination — they are deliberately sparse.

| What | Details |
|---|---|
| **FamilySearch collection** | "Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820–1891" — Collection ID 1784312 — ~1.2M indexed records across full date range |
| **FamilySearch volume (1840–1850)** | imageGroup 003345210 — 389 images — **3% name-indexed, but full-text searchable** — browse image by image or use full-text search; a standard name search will miss most arrivals |
| **Also on** | Ancestry.com (subscription) |
| **Physical originals** | National Archives at Boston, 380 Trapelo Rd, Waltham MA 02452 (NARA RG 36) |

> ⚠️ **Critical access note:** The 1840–1850 volume on FamilySearch is only 3% name-indexed. A clean name-search failure does NOT mean your ancestor didn't arrive through Boston. Use full-text search and browse the images directly.

---

## Massachusetts Alien Passenger Act Records (1837–1851)

Massachusetts ran its *own* parallel system under an 1837 state law requiring vessel masters to post bonds for passengers likely to become public charges. State inspectors generated supplementary lists that sometimes capture details absent from federal manifests — especially for passengers in poor health. These records are at the **Massachusetts State Archives** (Columbia Point, Boston) and are largely **physical only**.

---

## Naturalization records — essential bridge to origins

Pre-1906, any court of record could naturalize. Boston immigrants could file in:
- U.S. Circuit or District Court (District of Massachusetts)
- Suffolk County Court of Common Pleas
- Supreme Judicial Court
- Municipal Court of the City of Boston

**The Declaration of Intention ("first papers")** is often the most genealogically rich document — filed shortly after arrival, it records arrival date, port, vessel name, and country of origin. Even if naturalization was never completed, the declaration may still survive.

| Resource | Details |
|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | "Massachusetts, Naturalization Records, 1790–1906" — Collection ID 1459213 — ~346,000 records — **Indexed + images** |
| **NARA microfilm M1368** | *Index to New England Naturalization Petitions, 1791–1906* — all courts, one alphabet |
| **Physical** | NARA at Boston (federal courts); Massachusetts State Archives (state courts) |

---

## The Boston Pilot "Missing Persons" database — unique to Irish Famine research

This source has no parallel anywhere in 1840s immigration research:

- *The Pilot* (Boston's Irish Catholic newspaper) published thousands of "Information Wanted" notices, 1831–1921, placed by immigrants seeking lost relatives.
- Notices frequently name the **specific Irish county and townland of origin** — information that appears nowhere in the customs lists.
- Fully searchable, **free**, at **[infowanted.bc.edu](http://infowanted.bc.edu)**
- Search by the name of the *person sought* OR the *person placing the notice* — both are indexed.

---

## Vital records

Massachusetts began **statewide civil registration on 1 January 1842** — making it one of the earliest U.S. states to do so, though early compliance was uneven.

| Collection | FamilySearch ID | Records | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890 | 2061550 | ~3.5M | Indexed + images |
| Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920 | 1921657 | ~988,000 | Indexed + images |

**Before 1842 or for unregistered events:** Catholic parish registers are essential. The **Cathedral of the Holy Cross**, **St. Patrick's (East Boston)**, and **St. Mary's (North End)** all have records from the 1840s. Contact the **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** (66 Brooks Drive, Braintree, MA 02184) for access; some registers are also on FamilySearch microfilm.

---

## Census records

| Year | Coverage | Key value |
|---|---|---|
| **1840** | Heads of household named; others by age/sex brackets | Confirms presence; limited for immigrants |
| **1850** | Every household member named, with age and **country of birth** | First census to name all members; essential anchor document |

Both are **indexed + images**, free on FamilySearch. The 1850 **mortality schedule** (deaths June 1849 – June 1850) is separately indexed and covers the cholera year.

---

## Other high-value record types

| Record type | Where | Access level |
|---|---|---|
| **Boston city directories** (annual, 1789+) | Ancestry (indexed); Internet Archive (browse) | Subscription / Free |
| **Newspapers** (*The Pilot*, *Boston Daily Advertiser*, etc.) | Newspapers.com; Chronicling America; BPL microfilm | Subscription / Free |
| **Probate records, Suffolk County** | FamilySearch Coll. 1678901 (~568K records) | Indexed + images |
| **Suffolk County deeds** | masslandrecords.com (index); Registry in-person | Online index, physical originals |
| **Boston poll & property tax assessments (1840s)** | City of Boston Archives, 201 Rivermoor St | **Physical only** |
| **Deer Island quarantine registers** | Massachusetts State Archives; City of Boston Archives | **Physical only** |

---

## Repository summary

| Repository | What to go there for |
|---|---|
| **National Archives at Boston** (Waltham, MA) | Original passenger lists, federal court naturalizations, census originals |
| **Massachusetts State Archives** (Columbia Point, Boston) | State vital records (1842+), Alien Passenger Act records, state court naturalizations |
| **City of Boston Archives** (West Roxbury) | Tax assessments, Board of Health registers, quarantine records |
| **NEHGS / AmericanAncestors.org** (99 Newbury St, Boston) | Vital records, church registers, city directories, Pilot database |
| **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** (Braintree) | Catholic parish registers for all Boston parishes |
| **Boston Public Library, Microtext Dept.** | Newspaper microfilm (*The Pilot*, Advertiser, Post, Herald) |

---

## Key research tips

1. **The name-index for 1840s passenger lists is nearly empty** (3%) — always browse images or use full-text search; don't rely on a name index hit or miss.
2. **Search the Boston Pilot Missing Persons database first** for Irish families — it's the only source likely to give you the specific Irish townland.
3. **Naturalization declarations of intent** were often filed within months of arrival and record the vessel, arrival date, and country of origin — even if naturalization was never completed.
4. **The 1850 census is your best pivot point** — the first to name every household member. Work backward from it using city directories, tax lists, and church records.
5. **Search variant spellings** — Irish and other immigrant surnames were recorded phonetically; use wildcards and Soundex on FamilySearch.
6. **Check Liverpool departure records** — many Irish Famine emigrants sailed Liverpool → Boston. If the Boston list shows Liverpool as port of departure, look for the outbound Liverpool emigration record on Ancestry or FindMyPast for the origin voyage.
7. **Examine the full manifest folio**, not just your ancestor's line — family members, neighbors, and village associates frequently traveled together and appear on adjacent lines.

# Locality Guide: Indian Territory / Oklahoma — Native American Tribal Records (c. 1820–1907)

## Jurisdiction overview

**The most critical fact for this research:** Indian Territory had no county structure and no state government during the mid-1800s. Records were created by three overlapping authorities — **tribal governments**, the **Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA)**, and **federal courts** — not by counties or Oklahoma state offices. Oklahoma statehood didn't come until 1907. Do not search county-level Oklahoma records for this period; they did not yet exist.

The Five Civilized Tribes — **Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Creek (Muscogee), and Seminole** — were forcibly relocated from the southeastern U.S. to Indian Territory under the Indian Removal Act of 1830 and the Trail of Tears (1830s–1840s). Dozens of other tribes (Osage, Quapaw, Ottawa, Delaware, Shawnee, and others) also held land in the Territory.

---

## The record types, ranked by genealogical importance

### 1. Tribal Enrollment Rolls — the foundational series

These are organized by tribe, not by county. You must know which tribal nation your ancestor belonged to before most records become accessible.

#### Dawes Rolls (1898–1914)
The most widely used record set. Compiled by the Dawes Commission to assign land allotments, they enumerate the Five Civilized Tribes by name, age, sex, blood quantum, tribal affiliation, and enrollment card number. Categories include Citizens by Blood, Citizens by Intermarriage, Freedmen (formerly enslaved persons), and Mississippi Choctaws.

- **Digitization level:** ✅ **Indexed + images**
- **FamilySearch:** Collection **"Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959"** (ID 1987432) — 567,890 records, 283,945 images
- **Also on:** Ancestry.com (fully indexed)
- **Originals:** National Archives at Fort Worth, TX (NARA RG 75)

> The Dawes Rolls document the population c. 1898–1906, not the mid-1800s — but each entry references *earlier* rolls and gives an age that projects births back to the 1830s–1870s. The individual **Enrollment Card (jacket file)** at NARA Fort Worth may contain family testimony, birth dates, and relationship statements that are the richest genealogical documents for this era.

#### Earlier Mid-1800s Tribal Rolls (the true period sources)
| Roll | Date | Tribe | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Henderson Roll | 1835 | Cherokee | Pre-removal, eastern Cherokee |
| Armstrong Roll | 1831 | Choctaw | Removal-era |
| Duval Roll | 1832 | Creek | Removal-era |
| Old Settler Cherokee Roll | 1851 | Cherokee | Cherokees who migrated west before 1839 |
| Drennen Roll | 1852 | Cherokee | Post-reunification citizenship roll |
| Cooper Roll | 1855 | Choctaw | |
| Chickasaw Census Roll | 1878 | Chickasaw | |
| Creek Census | 1890 | Creek | |

- **Digitization level:** ✅ **Indexed + images** (many) via FamilySearch collection 1987432 and Ancestry; some remain **microfilm only** at NARA
- **Volume found:** FamilySearch holds one digitized volume of **Tribal Enrollment Cards, 1840–1870** (image group 002298871; 940 images; **99% name-indexed and full-text searchable**)

---

### 2. Annuity Payment Rolls (1830s–1900s)

Compiled annually by BIA agents, these list tribal heads of household (sometimes full families) entitled to payments under treaty obligations. They function as an annual census substitute for the years between formal rolls.

- **Where held:** NARA RG 75, Microcopy **M234** (Letters Received by the Office of Indian Affairs, 1824–1880), and successor series; physical at NARA Fort Worth
- **Digitization level:** ⚠️ **Partially indexed** on FamilySearch and Fold3; much of M234 is **browse-only microfilm**
- **How to access:** Search the FamilySearch Catalog under "Oklahoma — Native Races" or "United States — Native Races" for specific microfilm roll numbers

---

### 3. Treaty Records

Federal treaties often include schedules of signatories and sometimes family groupings. The full text of all ratified Indian treaties is published in Kappler's *Indian Affairs: Laws and Treaties* — freely searchable online at the **Oklahoma State University digital library** (digital.library.osstate.edu/kappler).

Key treaties for Indian Territory:
- Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek, 1830 (Choctaw removal)
- Treaty of Cusseta, 1832 (Creek removal)
- Treaty of New Echota, 1835 (Cherokee removal — contested)
- Treaties of 1866 (post–Civil War; established Freedmen citizenship)

- **Digitization level:** Treaty texts **online, full-text** (Kappler); original NARA documents **physical only** (RG 75)

---

### 4. BIA Agency Records (NARA Record Group 75)

The central federal record group for Native American genealogy. Held primarily at the **National Archives at Fort Worth, TX**, which is *the* critical repository for this research.

Key series:
- **M234** — Letters Received, Office of Indian Affairs (includes tribal censuses embedded in correspondence)
- **M1186** — Records of the Cherokee Indian Agency
- Records of the Five Civilized Tribes Agency (post-1890)
- Birth and Death Registers kept by agents (post-c. 1900)
- School enrollment records

- **Digitization level:** Mostly **physical only** or **browse-only microfilm**; some series partially indexed on **Fold3**
- **Access:** NARA online catalog at archives.gov/fort-worth; NARA's Native American research portal at archives.gov/research/native-americans

---

### 5. Land and Allotment Records

Records of land assignments to individual tribal members under the Dawes allotment program (Curtis Act, 1898). Each allottee's case file is tied to their Dawes Roll number and may contain family testimony and relationship statements.

- **Digitization level:** ✅ **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch **"Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907"** (Collection ID 1541872; 234,567 records, 117,283 images)
- **Also:** BLM General Land Office (glorecords.blm.gov) for federal patents; allotment case files (physical) at NARA Fort Worth

---

### 6. Missionary and Church Records (1820s onward)

Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, and Moravian missionaries were active in Indian Territory from the 1820s. They kept registers of converts, baptisms, marriages, and burials — often the only vital records for this era. Mission school enrollment lists are a parallel source.

- **Digitization level:** ✅ **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch **"Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965"** (Collection ID 1654321; 123,456 records, 61,728 images)
- **Physical:** Presbyterian records at the Presbyterian Historical Society (Philadelphia); Baptist records at the American Baptist Historical Society; Cherokee Baptist church records at the Cherokee Nation archives

---

### 7. Vital Records (Civil Registration)

**There were none during this period.** Oklahoma had no civil registration of births, marriages, or deaths before 1907. Some tribal governments maintained marriage registers:
- **Cherokee Nation marriage records (1838–1900)** — among the best-preserved; held at the Oklahoma Historical Society
- Choctaw and Creek nations also kept marriage records

**Substitutes:** Missionary registers, annuity rolls, Dawes Roll applications, allotment case files

---

### 8. Census Records

- **Federal census:** Indian Territory was **excluded from U.S. federal enumerations through 1880** — the 1840, 1850, 1860, 1870, and 1880 censuses do not cover the Five Civilized Tribes area. Oklahoma Territory (western half, opened 1889) first appears in 1890 and 1900.
- **1900 and 1910 federal census:** The Five Civilized Tribes area was finally enumerated; cross-reference with the Dawes Rolls for full identification.
- **1890 destruction:** The 1890 population schedules were largely destroyed by fire; Oklahoma Territory fragments are minimal.
- **BIA tribal censuses:** Annual or periodic enumerations compiled by BIA agents — in NARA RG 75 and partially available via FamilySearch collection 1987432.

---

### 9. Military Records

The Civil War saw major fighting in Indian Territory, with the Five Civilized Tribes split between Confederate and Union allegiances.

- **Confederate units:** 1st–3rd Cherokee Mounted Rifles, Choctaw and Chickasaw Mounted Rifles, Creek and Seminole regiments
- **Union units:** 1st–3rd Indian Home Guard regiments
- **Access:** Service records and pension files at NARA Washington, D.C.; partially indexed on **Fold3** (fold3.com). Pension files are genealogically rich — widow's claims often include marriage and family information.

---

### 10. Tribal Court and Legislative Records

The Five Civilized Tribes each operated court systems with civil and criminal jurisdiction over tribal members: trial transcripts, guardianship files, citizenship court decisions.

- **Where held:** Oklahoma Historical Society (OHS), Oklahoma City — physical volumes; some finding aids online at okhistory.org/research
- **Citizenship court cases** (post-1896): Adjudicated disputed tribal membership claims; case files at NARA Fort Worth can include extensive family evidence

---

### 11. Freedmen Records

After the Treaties of 1866, enslaved persons held by tribal members were emancipated and enrolled as tribal citizens on separate Freedmen schedules of the Dawes Rolls.

- **Access:** Freedmen Dawes Roll entries in FamilySearch collection 1987432 and on Ancestry
- **Freedmen's Bureau records:** NARA RG 105; partially indexed on Fold3; originals at NARA Fort Worth

---

### 12. Tribal Newspapers

Several tribes published newspapers that are valuable for obituaries, legal notices, and community news:
- *Cherokee Advocate* (1844–1906, Tahlequah) — held at the Oklahoma Historical Society; partial digitization
- *Choctaw Telegraph* and others

- **Access:** OHS Digital Collections for *Cherokee Advocate*; Chronicling America (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) has limited Indian Territory coverage

---

## Repository guide

### Online

| Repository | What's there | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Collections 1987432, 1541872, 1654321, 2134789; Tribal Enrollment Cards vol. 002298871 | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | Dawes Rolls; U.S. Federal Census (1900, 1910) | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Civil War Indian regiment service records; BIA/pension records | Subscription |
| **BLM GLO** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents | Free |
| **OSU Kappler Library** (digital.library.okstate.edu/kappler) | Full text of all ratified Indian treaties | Free |
| **NARA Catalog** (archives.gov/fort-worth) | Finding aids for RG 75 | Free |

### Physical

| Repository | Key holdings |
|---|---|
| **NARA Fort Worth, TX** | RG 75 — Dawes Rolls originals, allotment case files, annuity rolls (M234), agency correspondence, school records, citizenship court files |
| **NARA Washington, D.C.** | Military service and pension records; ratified Indian treaties |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society, Oklahoma City** | Tribal legislative and court records; *Cherokee Advocate*; Indian Pioneer Papers (WPA oral histories) |
| **Cherokee Nation Archives, Tahlequah** | Marriage registers, citizenship rolls, council minutes |
| **Choctaw / Chickasaw / Creek / Seminole Nation Archives** | Tribal records — contact each nation's cultural preservation office |
| **Thomas Gilcrease Institute, Tulsa** | Indian Territory manuscript collections |
| **University of Oklahoma, Western History Collections, Norman** | Maps, photographs, manuscript collections |

---

## Records NOT online (exist but require physical access or microfilm)

- Pre-1870 BIA agency correspondence and census lists (M234) — browse-only microfilm, not name-indexed
- Tribal court records at OHS — physical volumes only
- Individual allotment case files at NARA Fort Worth — not digitized; requested by Dawes Roll number
- Mission school enrollment records — denominational archives, not digitized
- Tribal marriage registers (Cherokee, Choctaw, Creek) — OHS, not systematically digitized

---

## Known record losses and gaps

| Gap | Substitute |
|---|---|
| Indian Territory excluded from 1840–1880 federal census | BIA annuity rolls, tribal census rolls, missionary registers |
| 1890 federal census destroyed | 1890 BIA tribal censuses; Dawes Roll age projections |
| Pre-removal eastern records lost or incomplete | Research Georgia, Alabama, Mississippi, Tennessee archives for the ancestor's eastern home |
| Civil War disruption of agency records | Military pension files; post-war tribal censuses; 1866 treaty schedules |

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with the Dawes Rolls, then work backward** — they anchor ages to birth decades and reference earlier rolls; the enrollment card jacket file at NARA Fort Worth is the richest individual document.

2. **Identify the tribe first.** Records are organized by tribe, not by county. Mixed-blood and intermarried individuals may appear in multiple tribal record sets.

3. **Freedmen research requires the Freedmen schedules** — separate from the citizen-by-blood rolls; also check Freedmen's Bureau records (RG 105).

4. **The Indian Pioneer Papers (OHS) are underused.** The WPA in the 1930s collected ~11,000 oral history interviews from Oklahoma pioneers — many Native Americans recalling mid-1800s events in detail.

5. **Check the pre-removal eastern state** before researching Indian Territory. Cherokee ancestors: Georgia and Tennessee. Choctaw: Mississippi. Creek: Alabama. Chickasaw: Mississippi/Tennessee. Seminole: Florida.

6. **NARA's Native American research portal** (archives.gov/research/native-americans) provides finding aids specific to RG 75.

7. **FamilySearch wiki articles to consult:**
   - *"Oklahoma Native American Records"* — familysearch.org/en/wiki/Oklahoma_Native_American_Records
   - *"Five Civilized Tribes Records"* — familysearch.org/en/wiki/Five_Civilized_Tribes_Records

8. **DNA testing** can complement documentary research where tribal enrollment records are disputed or ambiguous.

---

# Locality Guide: Pennsylvania Quaker Communities (c. 1750–1820)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Formed:** Pennsylvania founded 1682 by William Penn as a Quaker refuge; U.S. state 1787.
- **Administrative center:** Philadelphia (colonial capital); Lancaster (1799); Harrisburg (1812).
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Colony of Pennsylvania → Commonwealth of Pennsylvania after 1776.
- **Economy:** Mercantile trade (Philadelphia); grain farming (Chester, Bucks, Lancaster); early ironworks.
- **Ethnic/religious composition:** English Quakers dominant in Philadelphia, Chester, Bucks, and Delaware counties. German Lutheran/Reformed/Mennonite in Lancaster, York, Berks. Scotch-Irish Presbyterian in western counties.
- **Key historical events:** French and Indian War (1754–1763); American Revolution (1775–1783) — profoundly disruptive for Quakers due to pacifism; Pennsylvania Constitutions of 1776 and 1790.

---

## Boundary changes

- **Delaware County** carved from Chester County in **1789** — events before that date fall under Chester County records.
- **Montgomery County** carved from Philadelphia County in **1784** — earlier records are in Philadelphia County.
- Various western/central counties formed during this period as settlement expanded.
- **Implication:** Always identify the county at the time of the event, not the modern county. For Quaker research, a monthly meeting's quarterly meeting affiliation also shifted as new meetings were established.

---

## Available record types

### Quaker meeting records *(primary focus — most critical source for this period)*

Pennsylvania Quakers operated within a hierarchical structure: **preparative meetings** → **monthly meetings** (primary record-keeping unit) → **quarterly meetings** → **Philadelphia Yearly Meeting** (the governing body for Pennsylvania, New Jersey, Delaware, and parts of Maryland and New York).

**Record types kept by monthly meetings:**

| Record type | What it documents | Research value |
|---|---|---|
| **Birth registers** | Name, parents, date and place of birth (often recorded retrospectively) | Substitutes for civil birth records — no statewide PA registration until 1906 |
| **Marriage registers & certificates** | Both parties, parents, date, meeting; certificates signed by all witnesses | Primary marriage record for the period; witness names identify community members |
| **Death/burial registers** | Name, date, sometimes age and burial place | Substitutes for civil death records |
| **Monthly meeting minutes** | Disownments, removal certificates issued/received, disciplinary proceedings | Reveals migration, family conflicts, military-service violations |
| **Certificates of removal** | Issued when a member moved to another meeting — names member, family, destination | Traces migration between counties or to Ohio/Indiana after 1800 |
| **Disownment proceedings** | Members "disowned" for marrying outside the meeting, military service, oath-taking | Especially common 1775–1783; a "missing" ancestor may have been disowned |
| **Sufferings records** | Fines and property distraints for refusing militia service or war taxes | Identifies conscientious objectors; documents family economic circumstances |
| **Quarterly and yearly meeting minutes** | Broader governance, epistle correspondence | Context for community events; appeals of disownment decisions |

**Core Quaker counties for late 1700s Pennsylvania:**
- **Philadelphia** — Philadelphia Monthly Meeting (Northern and Southern preparative districts)
- **Bucks County** — Falls, Wrightstown, Buckingham, Richland, Horsham monthly meetings
- **Chester County** — Concord, Kennett, Bradford, Uwchlan, Sadsbury, London Grove monthly meetings
- **Delaware County** (formed 1789 from Chester) — Concord MM was the anchor meeting
- **Montgomery County** (formed 1784 from Philadelphia) — Gwynedd, Abington monthly meetings

**Digitization levels:**
- FamilySearch volume search returned **one digitized but unindexed volume**: Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes, 1750–1800 (image group 001188322, 145 images). `recordSearchablePercent: 0`, `fulltextSearchable: false` → **browse-only images**, accessible image by image only.
- **Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985** (FamilySearch Collection 1401638 — 1,245,830 records, 982,415 persons, 607,220 images) includes Quaker meeting records alongside other denominations — **indexed + images**, searchable by name.
- Many individual monthly meeting registers have been microfilmed by the FHL but exist only as **microfilm**, not in the broad indexed collection. Search the FamilySearch Catalog under "[County], Pennsylvania > Church Records > Society of Friends" for specific film numbers.

*(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Pennsylvania Church Records — Quaker (Friends) Records" and "Published Quaker Abstracts")*

---

### Civil vital records

- **No statewide civil registration until 1906.** For the late 1700s, there are no civil birth or death records.
- **County marriage bonds/licenses** were kept at the county courthouse from the colonial period — but **Quakers did not obtain civil marriage licenses**; their marriages were conducted entirely within the meeting. County courthouse marriage records will generally NOT contain Quaker marriages.
- **FamilySearch collections overlapping this period:**
  - Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950 — Collection 1571957 (312,045 records, 156,023 images) — **indexed + images**
  - Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900 — Collection 1502909 (198,765 records, **no images** — index only)

*(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Pennsylvania Vital Records — Overview")*

---

### Census and tax records

- **1790 federal census** — First U.S. census; heads of household named, others counted by age/sex. **Indexed + images** on FamilySearch and Ancestry.
- **1800, 1810, 1820 federal censuses** — Same format; all available indexed online.
- **No federal census before 1790.** Pennsylvania had no regular state census.
- **Tax records as pre-1790 substitutes:** Pennsylvania assessment lists and tax duplicates survive from the **1690s** in some counties. The 1693 Chester County tax list is among the earliest surviving colonial tax records. These name male heads of household (and sometimes widows) and are the best annual locating tool for the pre-1790 gap. Pennsylvania State Archives and county historical societies hold most of these; many have been published as abstracts.
- **Quaker note:** Men who refused to pay war taxes (1775–1783) may appear in meeting sufferings records but be absent or flagged in tax lists.

*(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Pennsylvania Land and Probate Records")*

---

### Probate and court records

- County **Register of Wills** holds probate records (wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships) from each county's formation date onward.
- **FamilySearch collection:** Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994 — Collection 1417683 (**893,214 records, 446,607 images** — **indexed + images**). Fully covers the late 1700s.
- Probate records are especially valuable for Quaker families: they name heirs, executors, and witnesses — often fellow meeting members — and establish family relationships when vital records are absent.
- **Quaker note:** Quakers commonly named fellow meeting members as bondsmen and executors, making probate records a community-mapping tool.

---

### Land records

- **Survey system:** Pennsylvania is a **state-land (metes-and-bounds) state** — land originally granted by William Penn, not the federal government. No township/range system.
- **Pennsylvania Land Office, Harrisburg:** Holds warrant books, survey books, and patent books from 1681 onward. These establish original ownership.
- **County deed books** (Recorder of Deeds) record subsequent transfers from each county's formation date. Deeds name adjacent landowners — useful for identifying neighbors, relatives, and fellow meeting members.
- **Implication for Quaker research:** Land records establish physical location, which then identifies which monthly meeting had jurisdiction. Neighbors named in deeds are often community members.

---

### Military records

**Revolutionary War (1775–1783):**
- **Official Quaker position:** The Society of Friends prohibited participation in the war on either side. The period 1775–1783 produced a **major wave of disownments** — recorded in monthly meeting minutes — for enlisting, paying war taxes, or swearing loyalty oaths. This is itself a genealogical record.
- **"Free Quakers" (Fighting Quakers):** Those disowned for war service sometimes formed their own meeting; the Free Quaker Meeting in Philadelphia (est. 1783) kept its own records, held at Friends Historical Library.
- **Continental Army service records and pensions:** NARA holds service records (online via Fold3, Ancestry). Revolutionary War pension applications (NARA Series M804) are on Fold3 and FamilySearch — these often contain detailed family narratives.
- **Pennsylvania militia records:** Pennsylvania State Archives.
- **Quaker conscientious objectors:** Meeting **sufferings records** document property distraints and fines — the primary record for Quakers who did not serve. These are at Friends Historical Library.

---

### Cemetery records

- Quaker meetings maintained their own **meetinghouse burial grounds**. 18th-century practice often used plain, uniform stones — or no stones at all.
- **Burial registers** (when they survive) are held by the monthly meeting's successor records at Friends Historical Library or Haverford College — largely **physical only**.
- Many Quaker burial grounds have been transcribed by local genealogical societies; published transcriptions may be more complete than FindAGrave.
- **Note:** Absence from a cemetery index does not mean a person was not buried there — early Quaker practice sometimes left burials unmarked.

---

### Newspaper records

- Philadelphia had active newspapers from the colonial period: *Pennsylvania Gazette* (1728–), *Pennsylvania Packet* (1771–), *Pennsylvania Journal* (1742–).
- **Quaker marriages were not typically announced in secular newspapers** — the meeting register is the authoritative record.
- Newspapers are useful for legal notices (land sales, estate settlements) and for non-Quaker neighbors and associates.
- **Chronicling America** (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov — free) and **Newspapers.com** (subscription) cover some Pennsylvania colonial and early national newspapers.

---

### Immigration records

- Most Pennsylvania Quaker families in the late 1700s were established — the major Quaker immigration waves occurred 1682–1720.
- **Certificates of removal from England or Ireland** issued by English/Irish meetings to emigrating members may survive in the receiving Pennsylvania meeting's papers at Friends Historical Library.
- Philadelphia was the primary port of entry; colonial passenger lists for English/Welsh Quaker arrivals are fragmentary but some have been published.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collections for this place and period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985 (Coll. 1401638 — 1.24M records incl. Quaker meeting records); Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994 (Coll. 1417683 — 893K records); Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950 (Coll. 1571957); Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900 (Coll. 1502909, index only); Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes 1750–1800 (image group 001188322, 145 images, browse-only) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census 1790–1820; Pennsylvania probate and land collections; some microfilmed Quaker meeting records | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Revolutionary War pension files (NARA M804); Continental Army records | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** (loc.gov) | Colonial and early national Pennsylvania newspapers | Free |
| **PA State Archives Digital Collections** (digitalarchives.state.pa.us) | Some colonial and early state records | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College** (Swarthmore, PA) | **Largest American Quaker archives:** monthly meeting minutes, birth/marriage/death registers, certificates of removal, sufferings records, Philadelphia Yearly Meeting records | In-person; some on FHL microfilm; friendshistoriclibrary.swarthmore.edu |
| **Quaker & Special Collections, Haverford College** (Haverford, PA) | Philadelphia Yearly Meeting records; strong colonial and early national holdings | In-person by appointment; haverford.edu/library/special |
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania** (Philadelphia, PA) | Broad PA manuscript collections; colonial Philadelphia records | In-person; hsp.org |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg, PA) | Land Office warrant/survey/patent books; colonial provincial records; tax assessment lists; militia records | In-person; archives.state.pa.us |
| **County courthouses / Register of Wills** | Probate files and deed books for each county from formation date | In-person or mail; most collections also on FamilySearch microfilm |
| **Family History Library** (Salt Lake City, UT) | Extensive Pennsylvania microfilm: monthly meeting registers, county deed and probate books | In-person or loan to local FamilySearch centers |

---

## Records NOT online

The following are known to exist and are relevant, but are not fully available in any online name-searchable database:

- **Individual monthly meeting registers** not included in FamilySearch Collection 1401638 — microfilmed by FHL but browse-only on microfilm; search the FamilySearch Catalog by county and denomination.
- **Monthly meeting minutes (narrative)** — largely unindexed; the Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes volume on FamilySearch (image group 001188322) is browse-only with no name index.
- **Certificates of removal (originals)** — held in receiving meeting papers at Friends Historical Library or Haverford; not digitized.
- **Sufferings records** — yearly and quarterly meeting sufferings books at Friends Historical Library; not online.
- **Pennsylvania Land Office warrant/survey/patent books** — originals at Pennsylvania State Archives; some microfilmed but not broadly indexed online.
- **Pre-1790 county tax assessment lists (most counties)** — at county archives and Pennsylvania State Archives; some published as abstracts by genealogical societies but not systematically searchable online.

---

## Known record losses

- No major catastrophic courthouse losses comparable to "burned counties" in other states — Pennsylvania courthouses generally survived intact.
- Some of the **earliest monthly meeting registers (pre-1700)** are fragmentary or lost; the oldest surviving Chester County meeting records date to the 1680s with gaps.
- **Substitute when Quaker records are missing:** County marriage bonds can confirm a marriage even if the meeting register is lost. Probate inventories name children and their spouses. Land deeds name parties and witnesses. The Hinshaw abstracts (below) may preserve data from registers since lost or damaged.

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with Hinshaw.** *William Wade Hinshaw's Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy*, Vol. 2 (1938), abstracts Philadelphia Yearly Meeting records — births, marriages, deaths, and disownments — organized by monthly meeting. It is one of the most important compiled sources for Pennsylvania Quaker ancestry and may preserve data from registers that are difficult to access today. Available at major genealogical libraries. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Pennsylvania Church Records — Published Quaker Abstracts")*

2. **Identify the monthly meeting first.** Every Quaker record is created by a specific monthly meeting. Determine which meeting had jurisdiction over where your ancestor lived (using county and township boundaries), then search the FamilySearch Catalog for that meeting's microfilmed records.

3. **Follow removal certificates.** Certificates of removal are the single best tool for tracing Quaker migration. If you lose a family in Pennsylvania, search Ohio and Indiana meeting records for incoming certificates citing the originating Pennsylvania meeting — many Pennsylvania Quakers migrated there after 1800.

4. **Check disownment records.** A "missing" ancestor after 1775 may have been disowned for Revolutionary War participation, marrying outside the meeting, or other violations. Disownment is recorded in meeting minutes. A disowned person's descendants may then appear in non-Quaker church records or county marriage bonds.

5. **The marriage approval process creates a paper trail.** Quaker marriages required the couple to appear before the monthly meeting twice (the "intentions"). The meeting appointed a committee to verify no impediments. This process is documented in minutes and creates multiple record entries per marriage — more evidence than a single register entry.

6. **Use tax lists for the 1750–1790 gap.** Pennsylvania county tax assessments, surviving from the 1690s in some counties, are the most consistent year-by-year locating tool before the 1790 federal census. Track a household across annual assessments to follow moves.

7. **Search the FamilySearch Catalog for specific meetings.** At familysearch.org/catalog, search by Place: "[County], Pennsylvania," browse to Church Records > Society of Friends. This reveals which specific monthly meeting films the FHL holds — even records not included in the broad indexed collection.

8. **Quaker date notation requires care.** Friends used a plain calendar — "First Month," "Second Month," etc. rather than January, February. Before the **1752 calendar change**, "First Month" was March, not January. Dates in Quaker records before 1752 may require conversion to the modern (Gregorian) calendar.

9. **Probate records map community networks.** Quakers commonly named fellow meeting members as executors, bondsmen, and witnesses. An inventory or will can reveal an entire network of community members, helping you identify neighbors whose records may corroborate or extend your research.

10. **Consult county historical and genealogical societies.** The Chester County Historical Society, Bucks County Historical Society, and equivalent organizations have published abstracted tax lists, deed indexes, and meeting record extracts that may cover gaps in what is online.

---

*Guide compiled using FamilySearch Wiki ("Pennsylvania Church Records," "Pennsylvania Vital Records," "Pennsylvania Land and Probate Records"), FamilySearch collections search and volume search results (June 2026), and Wikipedia summary on Quakers in Pennsylvania.*

Now let me load the MCP tools and run all jurisdictional and record searches simultaneously.I have all the data I need. Compiling and writing the guide now.Here is your locality guide for Pittsburgh / Allegheny County during the steel-industry boom:

---

# Locality Guide: Pittsburgh / Allegheny County, Pennsylvania (1860–1910)
**Focus: Occupational and industrial records for steel-industry families**

---

## Jurisdiction overview

**Allegheny County** is the governing jurisdiction for nearly all record types. Pittsburgh is the county seat. The county was formed in **1788** from Washington and Westmoreland counties and its boundaries were **stable throughout the 1860–1910 period** — no reorganizations affecting record custody.

**One important boundary note:** The independent City of Allegheny (across the Allegheny River) was **annexed by Pittsburgh in 1907**. Records created before annexation may be filed under *City of Allegheny* or *Allegheny City* — watch for this in census, directory, and vital records.

**Mill towns** along the Monongahela and Allegheny valleys — Homestead, Braddock, McKeesport, Duquesne, Carnegie — are all in Allegheny County and share the same county repositories, but had their own churches, newspapers, and directories. Search by borough name, not just "Pittsburgh."

**Population:** Pittsburgh alone grew from ~49,000 (1860) to ~322,000 (1900); the broader county approached 600,000 by 1900. This was driven almost entirely by steel.

**Ethnic composition:** Irish and German workers dominated before the Civil War. From the 1870s–1900s, a massive "new immigration" wave brought **Polish, Slovak, Croatian, Italian, Hungarian,** and Eastern European Jewish workers, plus growing African American migration.

---

## Occupational and industrial records ⭐ *The core focus*

These records are mostly **physical only** and fragmentary — but irreplaceable when they survive.

### Company employment records
| Source | Repository | Access |
|---|---|---|
| Carnegie Steel / U.S. Steel employment ledgers, payroll records | **Senator John Heinz History Center**, 1212 Smallman St., Pittsburgh | Physical only — contact for finding aids |
| Jones & Laughlin Steel records | Heinz History Center (partial) | Physical only |
| Mon Valley mill records (Homestead, Braddock, Duquesne) | **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area** | Physical only — contact before visiting |
| U.S. Steel Corporation records (partial) | **Hagley Museum and Library**, Wilmington, DE | Physical only |

> ⚠️ **Survival warning:** Most late-19th-century mill employment records were destroyed when plants closed or merged. Do not assume they exist. City directories are usually the best substitute.

### City directories ⭐ *Best occupational substitute*
Published **annually or biennially from 1813**, Pittsburgh city directories list each person's **name, occupation, and street address**. Tracking an ancestor across multiple directory years reveals career progression and address changes year by year — and fully bridges the **lost 1890 census**.
- **Complete run:** Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh (main branch), 4400 Forbes Ave.
- **Online:** Many years browsable free on Google Books and Archive.org. Ancestry has some years indexed. No complete indexed online set exists — expect to browse year by year. **Browse-only images** for most online years.

### State factory inspector reports
Pennsylvania's Bureau of Industrial Statistics published **Annual Reports from 1872** onward — naming factories, recording workforce size, wages, work conditions, and accident victims. Not indexed by worker name, but identify specific mills and their operations.
- **Repository:** Pennsylvania State Library (Harrisburg); Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh local history collection.
- **Online:** Many years free on Google Books. **Browse-only.**

### Coroner's inquest records ⭐ *Essential for industrial accident deaths*
Steel mill accidents were common and legally required an inquest. Records name: the deceased, **witnesses and coworkers**, the specific mill or furnace, and cause of death. Jury verdicts sometimes name foremen or supervisors.
- **Repository:** Allegheny County Division of Archives, 542 Forbes Ave., Pittsburgh. **Physical only.** Not indexed online.
- **Supplement:** Newspaper coverage of inquest proceedings often supplies additional detail (see Newspapers below).

### Union and labor records
The **Amalgamated Association of Iron, Steel and Tin Workers** (AA) was the dominant union through the 1890s. Records contain: member name, lodge number, admission date, occupation, and sometimes address or next of kin.
- **Repository:** **Historical Collections and Labor Archives, Penn State University** (Paterno Library, University Park). Also some materials at the Heinz History Center. **Physical only.**

### Fraternal / mutual aid society records ⭐ *Often contain the home village*
Immigrant steelworkers joined ethnic mutual aid societies — a critical safety net before PA workers' compensation (enacted 1915). Enrollment records typically include: name, date of birth, **home village in Europe**, spouse/beneficiary, and death benefit paid.

| Organization | Ethnicity | Archive location |
|---|---|---|
| Croatian Fraternal Union | Croatian | Pittsburgh (national HQ) |
| First Catholic Slovak Union (FCSU) | Slovak | Beachwood, OH |
| National Slovak Society | Slovak | McMurray, PA |
| Polish National Alliance | Polish | Chicago, IL |
| Polish Roman Catholic Union | Polish | Chicago, IL |
| Verhovay Aid Association | Hungarian | Pittsburgh |

All are **physical only** — contact each organization directly.

---

## Vital records

- **Births & deaths:** Allegheny County recorded these from **1870** (earlier than Pennsylvania's statewide 1906 mandate), but pre-1906 coverage is incomplete. County registers are at the **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg). **Physical only** for 1870–1905.
- **Death certificates, 1906–1968:** FamilySearch collection #2255317 — **4.2 million records, fully indexed with images (free)**. Post-1906 certificates record occupation, employer, birthplace of parents, and cause of death — extremely valuable for industrial workers.
- **Marriages from 1885:** FamilySearch collection #1921317 — **Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950** — 524,000 records, **indexed + images**. Earlier: compiled marriages index (no images) in collection #1502909.
- **Industrial accident deaths** may be better documented in coroner's inquest records than in the standard death register.

---

## Church records ⭐ *Often the most important record for immigrants*

Catholic immigrants attended **specific ethnic parishes** — a Slovak worker did not attend the Irish or German parish. Identifying the correct ethnic parish is the key to unlocking baptism and marriage registers that name the Old World home village.

- **Latin-rite Catholic** (Irish, German, Italian, Polish): **Catholic Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives**, 111 Blvd. of the Allies, Pittsburgh. Many original registers; in-person or written inquiry required. **Physical only** for most pre-1900 originals. FamilySearch has microfilmed some parishes — search the FamilySearch Catalog by place.
- **Byzantine / Greek-Catholic** (Slovak, Carpatho-Rusyn, Ukrainian): **Byzantine Catholic Diocese of Pittsburgh**. Separate from Latin-rite. Registers often in Church Slavonic or Slovak. **Physical only.**
- **FamilySearch collection #1401638 — Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985:** 1.2 million records, 982,000 persons, 607,000 images — **indexed + images** for included congregations. Not all Pittsburgh parishes are represented.

---

## Census records

| Census year | Key features for industrial research | Access |
|---|---|---|
| 1860 | Occupation for all adults | Ancestry (subscription); FamilySearch (free) |
| 1870 | Occupation; birthplace | Ancestry; FamilySearch |
| 1880 | Occupation; relationship to head | Free on FamilySearch and Ancestry |
| **1890** | **Destroyed** — almost entirely lost in 1921 fire | Substitute: city directories, tax lists, veterans' schedules fragment |
| **1900** | **Immigration year; years in US** — key for Old World searches | Free on FamilySearch |
| **1910** | Naturalized or alien; language; occupation and industry | Free on FamilySearch |
| **1880 Manufacturer's Schedules** | Lists specific mills by name, owner, workforce size — not indexed by worker name | Browse-only on FamilySearch/Ancestry |

---

## Naturalization records

Pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in **any court**. Both declarations of intention ("first papers") and petitions for citizenship are at the **National Archives at Philadelphia** (701 Market St.) on microfilm, covering the Allegheny County Court of Common Pleas, 1798–1930. Some indexed on Ancestry. **Microfilm / partially indexed.**

> **Tip:** Many immigrant steelworkers filed first papers years before petitioning. The declaration names birthplace and sometimes home village — check even if full citizenship was never completed.

---

## Newspapers

| Paper | Period | Online access |
|---|---|---|
| *Pittsburgh Gazette* / *Post-Gazette* | 1786–present | Newspapers.com (subscription), full-text |
| *Pittsburgh Post* | 1842–1920s | Newspapers.com (subscription) |
| *Pittsburgh Dispatch* | 1846–1920s | Chronicling America (free, partial) |
| *Jednota* (Slovak) | 1891–present | Slovak Institute, Cleveland (physical); partial scans |
| *Pittsburgh Courier* (African American) | 1907–present | ProQuest Historical Newspapers |

Obituaries in this era frequently name: surviving family members, church affiliation, **employer**, and home village. Industrial accident inquest coverage names coworkers.

---

## Immigration records

- Passenger lists (New York, Baltimore, Philadelphia) are **indexed + images** on FamilySearch and Ancestry. After 1895, manifests list a **destination contact** in Pittsburgh — often a brother or neighbor already working at a specific mill.
- **Port of Baltimore** was common for Eastern European immigrants via Hamburg-Amerika and North German Lloyd Lines.

---

## FamilySearch collections summary

| Collection | ID | Records | Access level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pennsylvania Death Certificates, 1906–1968 | 2255317 | 4.2 million | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985 | 1401638 | 1.2 million | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950 | 1921317 | 524,000 | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994 | 1417683 | 893,000 | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950 | 1571957 | 312,000 | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900 | 1502909 | 198,000 | Index only (no images) |

---

## Records NOT online

These exist but are absent from all indexed online databases:
- County birth and death registers, 1870–1905 (PA State Archives)
- Company employment and payroll records (Heinz History Center / Rivers of Steel)
- Coroner's inquest records (Allegheny County Archives)
- Annual tax assessment lists (Allegheny County Archives)
- Ethnic fraternal/mutual aid membership records (each organization's own archive)
- Most pre-1900 Catholic parish registers not microfilmed by FamilySearch (Diocese Archives)
- Byzantine/Greek-Catholic parish registers (diocesan archives)
- Union membership ledgers (Penn State Labor Archives)
- Most city directory years — browse-only at Carnegie Library or Archive.org

---

## Known record losses

- **1890 federal census** — almost entirely destroyed (1921 fire). Substitute with: city directories (1889 / 1891); tax assessment lists; the surviving veterans' schedules fragment.
- **Company records** — vast quantities of Carnegie Steel / U.S. Steel employment records were destroyed when plants closed, particularly post-WWII. Absence of records online does not mean the mill didn't keep them; it usually means they were destroyed.
- **No major courthouse fire** — unlike many Pennsylvania counties, Allegheny County has no catastrophic record loss comparable to burned courthouses elsewhere. Pre-1845 records survived the Great Pittsburgh Fire.

---

## Top research tips

1. **Start with 1880 and 1900 censuses.** The 1880 gives occupation; the 1900 adds immigration year to narrow your Old World search.
2. **Run city directories as an annual census** — especially critical to bridge the lost 1890 census and track address and occupational changes.
3. **Identify the specific ethnic parish**, not just the denomination. The parish register is the most direct path to the ancestral village.
4. **Cross-reference coroner's records with newspapers.** Mill accident inquest coverage names coworkers who may share your ancestor's village of origin.
5. **Check the 1880 Manufacturer's Census schedules** to identify which company employed your ancestor and cross-reference with surviving company records.
6. **Contact the Heinz History Center first** before any industrial research trip — their staff maintain finding aids for collections not fully searchable online.
7. **Fraternal society records may supply the home village** when nothing else will — enrollment affidavits often have village-level detail not found in any government record.

---

The guide has been saved to **`pittsburgh-allegheny-county-pa-locality-guide.md`** in your working folder. The **Heinz History Center** and **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** are your two highest-priority physical destinations for industrial-era families; for immigrant families, add the appropriate **diocesan archives** once you've identified the ethnic parish.

# Locality Guide: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania — 1793 Yellow Fever Epidemic (c. 1785–1810)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Formed:** Philadelphia County established 1682 by William Penn; Philadelphia City incorporated 1701. The city and county were separate jurisdictions (not consolidated until 1854), so both operated concurrently in 1793.
- **County seat:** Philadelphia City
- **Population:** ~28,500 (1790 census); ~50,000–55,000 by 1793 including suburbs and Northern Liberties. Approximately **5,000 people — one-tenth of the population — died in the epidemic**. An estimated 17,000–20,000 fled the city during August–November 1793.
- **Economy:** Seaport, trade, and finance; skilled artisan trades; printing. Philadelphia was the largest city in North America and the U.S. capital in 1793.
- **Ethnic composition:** English and Welsh Quakers (founding stock); large German and German-Swiss communities; Scots-Irish; Irish Catholic; Swedish (Delaware Valley settlers); French Huguenot; Sephardic Jewish. A significant free Black community (~2,000+) provided much of the epidemic nursing care.
- **Denominations:** Society of Friends; Anglican/Episcopal (Christ Church, St. Peter's); German Reformed; German Lutheran; Presbyterian; Methodist; Catholic (St. Joseph's, St. Mary's); Swedish Lutheran (Gloria Dei); Jewish (Mikveh Israel); African Episcopal (St. Thomas); African Methodist Episcopal (Mother Bethel, founded 1793).

---

## Boundary changes

Philadelphia County boundaries were stable throughout this period. However:

- **Northern Liberties** (north of Vine St.) and **Southwark** (south of South St.) were distinct townships within the county — not part of the city proper — but residents appear in the same church, tax, and relief records.
- **Germantown, Kensington, Passyunk Township**: separate county townships; pre-1854 records may be filed under the township.
- Subsequent yellow fever outbreaks struck in **1797, 1798, and 1799** — if your ancestor died in those years, the same record types apply.

---

## Available record types

### ⚠️ Epidemic-specific records (1793) — START HERE

These sources are unique to the outbreak and are the first stop for any family affected.

**1. Mathew Carey, *A Short Account of the Malignant Fever* (1793)**
The 4th edition lists names of the dead, organized by neighborhood. Digitized free on **HathiTrust** and **Google Books**.
**Digitization level: Indexed + full text online (free)**

**2. Citizens' Relief Committee minutes and reports**
Mayor Matthew Clarkson's committee documented individuals who received food, medicine, and nursing; orphaned children placed in care; and reported deaths. Manuscript originals at the **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)**.
**Digitization level: Physical only — HSP**

**3. Bush Hill Hospital records**
The city's improvised fever hospital, managed by Stephen Girard and Peter Helm. Admission and mortality records — fragmentary but extant — at **HSP**.
**Digitization level: Physical only — HSP**

**4. Weekly bills of mortality (newspapers, Aug–Nov 1793)**
Published weekly in the *Pennsylvania Gazette* and daily in *Dunlap's American Daily Advertiser*, the *Federal Gazette*, and the *General Advertiser* — death counts by ward, sometimes by name.
**Digitization level: Microfilm (Free Library of Philadelphia, HSP); partial free digitization at Chronicling America; full subscription coverage at Accessible Archives**

**5. *A Narrative of the Proceedings of the Black People* (Jones & Allen, 1794)**
Names Black Philadelphians who nursed the sick and some who died. Digitized free on HathiTrust. The African American caregiving community suffered significant casualties and is poorly represented in mainstream records.
**Digitization level: Full text online (free)**

---

### Vital records (civil registration)

Pennsylvania did **not** require statewide civil registration of births, marriages, or deaths until **1906**. There are **no government-issued death certificates for 1793**. Church records are the *only* systematic vital records for this period.

> The FamilySearch collection *Pennsylvania Death Certificates, 1906–1968* does not apply here.

---

### Church records — primary vital record source

Church burial registers are the functional equivalent of death certificates for 1793. Burial entries frequently note "yellow fever" or "malignant fever" as cause of death in late summer–fall 1793. A spike in burials from August through November 1793 is visible in virtually every Philadelphia congregation's register.

| Congregation | Records from | Language | Where held |
|---|---|---|---|
| Christ Church (Episcopal) | 1709 | English | HSP; FamilySearch; Ancestry |
| St. Peter's (Episcopal) | 1761 | English | HSP; FamilySearch |
| Gloria Dei / Old Swedes' (Lutheran) | 1697 | Swedish/English | HSP; FamilySearch microfilm |
| Philadelphia Monthly Meeting (Quakers) | 1682 | English | Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore |
| First Presbyterian | 1698 | English | Presbyterian Historical Society |
| St. Joseph's Catholic | 1757 | Latin/English | Archdiocese of Philadelphia Archives |
| St. Mary's Catholic | 1763 | Latin/English | Archdiocese of Philadelphia Archives |
| St. Michael's Lutheran (German) | c. 1745 | German | Lutheran Archives Center at Philadelphia |
| Zion Lutheran (German) | c. 1750 | German | Lutheran Archives Center at Philadelphia |
| St. George's Methodist | 1769 | English | St. George's Heritage Center |
| St. Thomas African Episcopal | 1792 | English | HSP |
| Mother Bethel AME | 1793 | English | Mother Bethel AME Church |
| Mikveh Israel (Jewish) | 1738 | Hebrew/English | Congregation Mikveh Israel; American Jewish Archives |

**FamilySearch collection:** *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* (Collection [1401638](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638)) — **1,245,830 records / 982,415 persons indexed / 607,220 images.** Covers many (not all) Philadelphia congregations. **Indexed + images.**

The **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania** has published transcribed indexes to many Philadelphia church records not yet on FamilySearch — check their *Publications* series.

**Language note:** German Lutheran and Reformed registers are in Kurrent script. Translation assistance may be needed.

---

### Census records

| Census | Year | Coverage | Digitization level |
|---|---|---|---|
| First Federal Census | 1790 | Heads of household only | Indexed + images — FamilySearch, Ancestry |
| Second Federal Census | 1800 | Heads of household + age brackets | Indexed + images — FamilySearch, Ancestry |

No Pennsylvania state census exists for this period. **Tax lists** (below) are the substitute for non-heads-of-household and inter-census years. Comparing 1790 and 1800 entries can reveal deaths (household members disappear) and remarriages.

---

### Probate and court records

The epidemic produced a **spike in probate filings** in late 1793 and throughout 1794. Philadelphia County Orphans' Court also appointed guardians for epidemic orphans — those files are particularly rich.

- **FamilySearch collection:** *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* (Collection [1417683](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683)) — **893,214 records, 446,607 images.** Includes Philadelphia County. **Indexed + images.**
- **Physical originals:** Philadelphia City Archives; Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg).
- Probate records from 1793–1795 often state "deceased of the malignant fever" and name surviving children, siblings, and creditors.

---

### City directories

An underused but powerful source for epidemic research. Compare entries immediately *before* and *after* the outbreak.

| Directory | Year | Access |
|---|---|---|
| Clement Biddle's *Philadelphia Directory* | 1791 | HathiTrust (free) |
| James Hardie's *Philadelphia Directory* | 1793 | HathiTrust (free) |
| James Hardie's *Philadelphia Directory* | 1794 | HathiTrust (free) |
| Edmund Hogan's *Prospect of Philadelphia* | 1795 | HathiTrust (free) |
| Stafford's *Philadelphia Directory* | 1797, 1798, 1801 | HathiTrust (free) |

**Research tip:** A surname disappearing between the 1793 and 1794 editions, or a widow's name replacing a man's at the same address, is strong circumstantial evidence of an epidemic death.

---

### Tax records

- Pennsylvania county tax assessments (real and personal property) were compiled annually.
- Comparing 1792–1793 and 1794–1795 assessments can reveal deaths and estate distributions.
- **Where held:** Philadelphia City Archives; Pennsylvania State Archives; some volumes on FamilySearch microfilm.
- **Digitization level:** Browse-only images on FamilySearch microfilm; largely physical at City Archives.
- Search the FamilySearch Catalog at [familysearch.org/search/catalog](https://www.familysearch.org/search/catalog) — place search: "Philadelphia, Pennsylvania."

---

### Newspaper records

Philadelphia papers published daily death counts, ward-level mortality tables, and individual notices throughout the epidemic.

| Newspaper | Period | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| *Pennsylvania Gazette* | 1728–1800 | Weekly bills of mortality Aug–Nov 1793 |
| *Dunlap's American Daily Advertiser* | 1791–1795 | Daily; most complete daily death counts |
| *Federal Gazette* | 1788–1798 | Daily; strong epidemic coverage |
| *General Advertiser* | 1790–1794 | Daily; later *Aurora General Advertiser* |

- **Accessible Archives** (subscription): most complete digital run of Philadelphia papers — [accessible.com](https://www.accessible.com)
- **Chronicling America** (free): [chroniclingamerica.loc.gov](https://chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) — search "yellow fever" + "Philadelphia," filtered to 1793
- **Microfilm:** Free Library of Philadelphia; HSP

---

### Almshouse, hospital, and institutional records

| Institution | Records | Where held | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Philadelphia Almshouse / Guardians of the Poor | Admission registers, relief rolls, apprenticeship indentures for orphans | Philadelphia City Archives; some at HSP | Physical |
| Pennsylvania Hospital (founded 1751) | Admission register, death register (did not treat yellow fever patients) | UPenn Health System Archives; some at HSP | Physical |
| Bush Hill Hospital (1793 only) | Fragmentary admission and death records | HSP (Committee papers) | Physical |

The Guardians of the Poor records are especially valuable for tracing **epidemic orphans** and **destitute widows** placed in institutional care in 1793–1795.

---

### Probate / Orphans' Court guardianship files

For families with children who lost one or both parents in 1793: Philadelphia County Orphans' Court guardianship records (1793–1800) are held at Philadelphia City Archives and on FamilySearch microfilm. They name the deceased parent, the child(ren), and the appointed guardian.

---

### Military records

- **Continental Army pension files** for Revolutionary War veterans living in Philadelphia in 1793: NARA; digitized on Fold3 (subscription) and Ancestry (subscription); some free on FamilySearch.
- **Pennsylvania militia records:** Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg.

---

### Immigration and naturalization

- Philadelphia was the principal port of entry for Pennsylvania immigrants.
- **Pre-1820 passenger lists** are sparse; Pennsylvania required alien registration from 1727 (the "Palatine lists" — published and indexed).
- **Pre-1906 naturalizations** were filed in any court of record: Philadelphia Court of Common Pleas, Philadelphia District Court.
- **Where held:** NARA Philadelphia; Pennsylvania State Archives; HSP.

---

### Cemetery records

Many epidemic victims were buried hastily; formal churchyard burial was not always possible at the crisis peak.

| Cemetery | Notes | Online coverage |
|---|---|---|
| Christ Church Burial Ground | Well-documented; many prominent Philadelphians | FindAGrave |
| Gloria Dei Churchyard | Oldest Protestant burial ground in PA | FindAGrave (partial) |
| Quaker burial grounds (multiple meetings) | Plain or no markers; registers at Friends Historical Library | Minimal on FindAGrave |
| Strangers' Burial Ground | City-run; used heavily during epidemic for non-church members | Records fragmentary at City Archives |
| Hebrew Burial Ground (Spruce St.) | Mikveh Israel congregation | Mikveh Israel; some on FindAGrave |

**FindAGrave:** [findagrave.com](https://findagrave.com) — transcriptions and photos for major Philadelphia churchyards. Coverage is uneven for 1793 epidemic victims (plain or missing markers are common).

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Relevant collections | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | PA Church and Town Records 1708–1985 (1.2M records); PA Probate Records 1683–1994 (893K records); PA Civil Marriages 1677–1950; PA Compiled Marriages 1700–1900; Federal Census 1790, 1800 | Free |
| **Ancestry** | PA church records; federal census 1790, 1800; Philadelphia passenger lists; PA court records | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Revolutionary War pension files for Philadelphia veterans | Subscription |
| **Accessible Archives** | Complete digital runs of Philadelphia newspapers 1790s | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America (LOC)** | Partial Philadelphia newspaper coverage | Free |
| **HathiTrust / Google Books** | Mathew Carey pamphlet; Jones & Allen *Narrative*; city directories 1791–1801; published church record transcriptions | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Key holdings | Contact |
|---|---|---|
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)** | Committee of Safety papers; Bush Hill records; church manuscripts; personal papers of Benjamin Rush and Matthew Clarkson; city directories | 1300 Locust St, Philadelphia — [hsp.org](https://hsp.org) |
| **Philadelphia City Archives** | Municipal records; Almshouse/Guardians of the Poor; deed books; tax records; city health officer records | 3101 Market St, Philadelphia — [phila.gov/archives](https://www.phila.gov/departments/department-of-records/city-archives/) |
| **Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore** | Philadelphia Monthly Meeting records (births, marriages, deaths) | Swarthmore, PA — [swarthmore.edu/friends-historical-library](https://www.swarthmore.edu/friends-historical-library) |
| **Presbyterian Historical Society** | First and Second Presbyterian Church registers | 425 Lombard St, Philadelphia — [history.pcusa.org](https://www.history.pcusa.org) |
| **Archdiocese of Philadelphia Archives** | St. Joseph's and St. Mary's Catholic registers | 222 N 17th St, Philadelphia — by appointment |
| **Lutheran Archives Center at Philadelphia** | German Lutheran congregation registers | Philadelphia — [lutheranarchives.org](https://www.lutheranarchives.org) |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives** | State land warrants; militia records; county records on microfilm | 350 North St, Harrisburg — [phmc.pa.gov](https://www.phmc.pa.gov) |
| **American Philosophical Society** | Papers of Benjamin Rush; epidemic medical correspondence | 104 S 5th St, Philadelphia — [amphilsoc.org](https://www.amphilsoc.org) |
| **NARA Philadelphia** | Federal court naturalization records | 900 Market St, Philadelphia |
| **Free Library of Philadelphia** | Newspaper microfilm; city directory collection | 1901 Vine St — [freelibrary.org](https://freelibrary.org) |

---

## Records NOT online

The following records **exist** but are **not** in any searchable online database. Do not assume their absence online means nonexistence:

- Citizens' Relief Committee manuscript minutes (1793) — HSP, physical
- Bush Hill Hospital admission and death register (1793) — HSP, physical
- Philadelphia Almshouse / Guardians of the Poor admission records (1790s) — City Archives, physical
- Philadelphia County Orphans' Court guardianship files (epidemic orphans, 1793–1800) — some on FS microfilm; many physical at City Archives
- Philadelphia deed books and ground rent records (1790s) — City Archives, largely physical
- Most German-congregation church registers — microfilmed at FamilySearch but **not name-indexed**; browse-only or physical at Lutheran Archives Center
- Philadelphia tax assessment books (1792–1796) — FS microfilm; not name-indexed as standalone collection
- Pennsylvania Hospital patient admission records (1793) — UPenn Health System Archives, physical
- Strangers' Burial Ground interment records (1793) — fragmentary at City Archives

---

## Known record losses

- **No major courthouse fires or archive losses** affect Philadelphia for this period. Unlike many counties, Philadelphia's primary repositories survived largely intact.
- **Burial markers:** Many 1793 victims have no surviving gravestone — plain Quaker markers, wooden markers, and mass epidemic burials left no physical trace. Church burial registers are the record of last resort.
- **Bush Hill records are fragmentary** — record-keeping was secondary to crisis management. Do not expect a complete patient roster.
- **Black community records are sparser**: St. Thomas African Episcopal registers survive at HSP; Mother Bethel AME records are fragmentary for 1793–1800.
- **Smaller congregations:** Some kept no register or registers were lost through neglect. Epidemic burials were sometimes recorded by whichever minister was available — check neighboring churches.

---

## Recommended research sequence

1. **Mathew Carey's pamphlet** (free, HathiTrust) — fastest single source for named epidemic dead.
2. **Church burial register** for your ancestor's denomination — FamilySearch Collection 1401638 first; then contact the denomination's archive if not found.
3. **Philadelphia County Probate Records** (FamilySearch 1417683) — search for wills or administrations filed late 1793 or 1794.
4. **City directories** — compare 1793 vs. 1794 editions (free, HathiTrust) for the household.
5. **Philadelphia newspapers** — Accessible Archives (subscription) or Chronicling America (free) for bills of mortality and death notices, August–November 1793.
6. **HSP** — for the Committee of Safety papers and Bush Hill records if your ancestor received relief or was hospitalized.
7. **Orphans' Court guardianship records** (City Archives or FS microfilm) — for epidemic orphans, 1793–1800.
8. **1790 and 1800 federal census** — compare to detect deaths (disappearing household members) and remarriages.
9. **Jones/Allen *Narrative*** (free, HathiTrust) + St. Thomas and Mother Bethel registers — for Black Philadelphia families.

---

## Key finding aids

- FamilySearch Wiki — Philadelphia Genealogy: [familysearch.org/en/wiki/Philadelphia_Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Philadelphia_Genealogy)
- Wikipedia — Yellow fever epidemic of 1793: [en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yellow_fever_epidemic_of_1793](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yellow_fever_epidemic_of_1793)
- HSP Research Guides: [hsp.org/research](https://hsp.org/research)
- Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania: [genpa.org](https://www.genpa.org)
