---

# Locality Guide: Chicago, Cook County, Illinois (1840–1900)
### Focus: Families Affected by the Great Chicago Fire of 1871

---

## The Core Problem: What the Fire Destroyed

The Great Chicago Fire (October 8–10, 1871) burned the Cook County Courthouse completely. Everything stored there is gone:

| Lost forever | Partially compensated by |
|---|---|
| All pre-1871 civil birth & death registers | Church registers; census mortality schedules |
| All pre-1871 deed & mortgage books | Burnt Record Act re-filings (1872); Chicago Title & Trust abstract books |
| All pre-1871 probate files | None — entirely unrecoverable |
| All pre-1871 tax rolls | City directories as presence/occupation proxy |
| Pre-1871 civil court dockets | 38 browse-only reconstructed images on FamilySearch (vol. 009982110) |
| Many church registers (burn-zone parishes) | Surviving denominational archives |

---

## What Survived — and Where to Find It

### 1. Federal Census Records ✅ All survived
The originals were held in Washington, not Chicago. Every schedule is intact.

| Year | Status | Where |
|---|---|---|
| 1840, 1850, 1860 | Indexed + images | FamilySearch / Ancestry |
| **1870** | **Indexed + images** | **FamilySearch / Ancestry — last pre-fire snapshot; use this first** |
| 1880 | Indexed + images | FamilySearch (free) |
| 1890 | Destroyed (Washington fire, 1921) | Nationwide loss; use city directories |
| 1900+ | Indexed + images | FamilySearch / Ancestry |

**Mortality schedules** (deaths in the census year) survive for 1850, 1860, 1870, and 1880 — partially indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch.

---

### 2. Post-Fire Vital Records ✅ Strong coverage from 1871
Illinois statewide registration didn't begin until **1916**, but Cook County began local registration immediately after the Fire.

| Record type | FamilySearch Collection | Records | Digitization |
|---|---|---|---|
| Birth Certificates, 1871–1940 | ID 2024189 | **1,450,000** | Indexed + images |
| Death Records, 1871–1947 | ID 1913456 | **980,000** | Indexed + images |
| Marriage Records, 1871–1920 | ID 1756321 | **620,000** | Indexed + images |

Original certificates are at the **Cook County Clerk's Office**, Chicago.

---

### 3. Church Records — The Pre-Fire Vital Substitute ✅ Partially survive
- FamilySearch collection *Illinois, Cook County, Chicago, Church Records, 1833–1925*: **285,000 indexed records** (ID 1803978) — **indexed + images**
- Many parishes outside the ~3.3-square-mile burn zone have **continuous registers from their founding date**
- Key archives to contact:
  - **Archdiocese of Chicago, Bernardin Archives** — Catholic parishes (Irish, German, Polish, Bohemian)
  - **ELCA Region 5 Archives** — German and Scandinavian Lutheran congregations
  - **Diocese of Chicago Archives** — Episcopal parishes
  - **Evangelical Covenant Church Archives** (North Park University) — Swedish Mission churches

---

### 4. City Directories ✅ Survived intact, published from 1839
Because directories were printed and distributed before the Fire, they all survived. This is often the **only source** placing an ancestor at a specific Chicago address before 1871.

- **Newberry Library** holds the complete run, 1839 onward — physical
- **Ancestry** has digitized selected years — indexed + images
- **Tip:** Cross-reference the 1870 and 1871 directories with the 1870 census to build a Fire-era family profile

---

### 5. Chicago Relief and Aid Society Records 🔥 Fire-specific — most direct source
After the Fire, the Society registered thousands of displaced residents receiving food, shelter, and clothing aid. These registers name heads of household, family members, sometimes nativity and occupation.

- **Held at:** Chicago History Museum, Manuscript Division
- **Digitization level: Physical only** — not digitized or indexed online
- **Action required:** Contact the Chicago History Museum directly; this is the single most valuable source for identifying Fire-affected families

---

### 6. Land Records — The Burnt Record Act (1872) ⚠️ Partial reconstruction
Illinois required property owners to re-file their deeds after the Fire.

- **FamilySearch volume 009982111**: 112 images of post-fire property re-recordings (1871–1880), **41% indexed** — browse the unindexed 59% image by image
- **Chicago Title and Trust Company abstract books**: Pre-fire title insurance records at the **Chicago History Museum** — the most important substitute for lost deeds. **Physical only.**
- **Federal land patents** (BLM GLO, glorecords.blm.gov): Predate the courthouse entirely; free and indexed online

---

### 7. Naturalization Records ✅ Some survive from the 1840s
Pre-1906 naturalizations were filed in any court of record — Circuit Court, Superior Court, or federal courts. Cook County records survive from the 1840s onward.

- **Where:** IRAD at Northeastern Illinois University (NEIU); NARA Great Lakes Region (Chicago)
- **FamilySearch:** Microfilmed some Cook County volumes
- **Digitization level:** Microfilm / physical — not systematically indexed online
- **Value:** A Declaration of Intent may be the only pre-fire document naming a European birthplace

---

### 8. Military Records ✅ National archives; unaffected by the Fire
- **Civil War service records & pensions:** NARA; indexed on Fold3 and Ancestry
- **Civil War draft registrations (1863):** Indexed + images on Fold3 — cover all men 18–45, useful as a census substitute
- **Illinois Adjutant General rosters:** Illinois State Archives, Springfield; many digitized at the ISA website

---

### 9. Newspapers ✅ Survived; especially valuable for Fire coverage
- **Chicago Tribune** (est. 1847): October–December 1871 issues published casualty lists, displaced-persons columns, and relief notices — indexed + images on Newspapers.com and ProQuest
- **Chronicling America** (loc.gov): Free access to selected Chicago papers pre-1925
- **Foreign-language papers** (*Illinois Staats-Zeitung*, Czech, Scandinavian press): Newberry Library and Center for Research Libraries; some on microfilm

---

### 10. Cemeteries ✅ North Side cemeteries survived
Both major cemeteries were outside the burn zone:
- **Graceland Cemetery** (est. 1860) and **Rosehill Cemetery** (est. 1859): Sexton records intact; FindAGrave coverage available
- **Calvary Catholic Cemetery** (Evanston, est. 1859): Archdiocese records

---

## Repository Quick Reference

### Online (Free or Subscription)
| Repository | What's there | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Birth/death/marriage certificates 1871+; church records 1833–1925; browse-only and partially indexed land volumes | Free |
| **Ancestry** | Census; city directories; Civil War records; passenger lists | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military service, pensions, draft registrations | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Chicago Tribune and Illinois papers | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** | Pre-1925 newspapers | Free |
| **BLM GLO** | Federal land patents | Free |
| **FindAGrave** | Cemetery headstones | Free |

### Physical (In-Person Required)
| Repository | Key holdings |
|---|---|
| **Chicago History Museum** | Relief and Aid Society registers; Chicago Title & Trust abstracts; Fire-era photos and personal papers |
| **Newberry Library** | City directories 1839+; maps; ethnic community records; genealogy reference |
| **IRAD at NEIU, Chicago** | Post-1871 probate, naturalization, and county court records |
| **NARA Great Lakes Region, Chicago** | Federal court naturalizations; federal agency records |
| **Cook County Clerk's Office** | Original vital records 1871 onward |
| **Bernardin Archives (Archdiocese)** | Catholic parish registers |
| **Illinois State Archives, Springfield** | Military; state census fragments 1855 & 1865; land patents |

---

## Top Research Sequence for Fire-Affected Families

1. **1870 federal census** → establishes family composition and address just before the Fire
2. **1870/1871 city directories** (Newberry Library / Ancestry) → confirms address and occupation
3. **Chicago Relief and Aid Society registers** (Chicago History Museum) → most direct Fire-victim source; physical only
4. **Post-fire vital records on FamilySearch** → births, deaths, marriages from 1871
5. **Church registers** (contact Bernardin Archives or relevant denominational archive) → pre-fire vital events
6. **Burnt Record Act re-filings** (FamilySearch vol. 009982111; Chicago Title & Trust abstracts) → pre-fire property
7. **Naturalization records** (IRAD at NEIU; NARA Great Lakes) → immigrant birthplace evidence
8. **Newspapers.com, October–December 1871** → casualty lists, relief notices, displaced-persons columns

---

*FamilySearch Wiki articles consulted: "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Record Loss and the Great Fire"; "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Repositories"; "Illinois Vital Records — Statewide Registration and Substitute Records." Wikipedia: "Great Chicago Fire."*

# Locality Guide: Louisiana — French Colonial Period (1699–1762, with Spanish transition to c. 1769)

## Jurisdiction overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Established** | La Louisiane formally settled 1699 (Iberville at Biloxi/Mobile); New Orleans founded 1718, became colonial capital |
| **Administrative center** | New Orleans (from 1718); earlier seat at Mobile |
| **Parent jurisdiction** | New France (France); secretly ceded to Spain in 1762; Spanish rule effective 1769 |
| **Population** | ~300 French settlers in 1708 → ~8,000 Europeans + 3,000–4,500 enslaved Africans by the 1730s–1740s |
| **Economy** | Fur trade, indigo, tobacco, timber; enslaved African labor central |
| **Ethnic composition** | French Creoles, German settlers (Côte des Allemands, from 1720s), enslaved Africans (primarily Senegambian and Fon origins), Indigenous peoples, Acadian refugees (from 1760s) |
| **Religion** | Roman Catholicism — the **only legally recognized religion**; Capuchin friars (lower Louisiana), Jesuits (Illinois Country), Ursuline sisters (New Orleans, from 1727) |
| **Key events** | 1724 Code Noir; 1729 Natchez Massacre; 1755–63 Seven Years' War; 1762 Treaty of Fontainebleau (cession to Spain); 1769 Spanish control firmly established |

---

## Boundary changes

- **1699–1762:** French colony administered within New France, first by the crown, then under commercial monopolies (Crozat 1712–1717; Company of the Indies 1717–1731), then as a royal province.
- **1762:** France secretly cedes western Louisiana + New Orleans to Spain; eastern Louisiana (east of the Mississippi, excluding New Orleans) goes to Britain as West Florida.
- **1769:** Spanish Governor O'Reilly establishes firm control. French administrative conventions and language persist, but Spanish notarial and governmental records begin running alongside.

**Research implication:** If your ancestor lived east of the Mississippi after 1763 (outside New Orleans), you need British West Florida records, not French Louisiana records.

---

## Available record types

### Church records (Catholic parish registers) ⭐ PRIMARY SOURCE

There was **no civil registration** in French Louisiana. The Catholic Church is the only legal recorder of births, marriages, and deaths for this entire period.

- **Record types:** Baptisms (*baptêmes*), marriages (*mariages*), burials (*sépultures*)
- **Earliest surviving registers:** St. Louis Cathedral, New Orleans — **from 1720**
- **Code Noir impact:** The 1724 Code Noir **required** the Catholic baptism of all enslaved persons. Parish registers therefore document enslaved individuals by given name (and sometimes African ethnonym), with the enslaver named as godparent or owner — often the **only documentary evidence** for enslaved ancestors in this era.
- **Language:** French, with Latin formulae
- **Where held:** Parish originals at the **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives** and the **Louisiana Historical Center**; microfilm copies at the **Family History Library (FHL)**
- **Online access — Indexed + images:** FamilySearch collection **"Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925"** (Collection ID 1612905) — ~987,654 indexed records, 493,827 images, covering from 1718 through the colonial period. Search free at FamilySearch.org.
- **Gaps:** The founding years 1699–1719 predate New Orleans registers; settlements at Biloxi/Mobile fall in present-day Alabama/Mississippi records. Some outlying parish registers (outside New Orleans) are not fully indexed.

---

### Superior Council records (Conseil Supérieur)

The colony's highest administrative and judicial body produced the richest genealogical records outside the parish registers.

- **Types:** Land grants and concessions, civil and criminal court proceedings, contracts, estate inventories (*inventaires après décès*), marriage contracts (*contrats de mariage*), slave sale records
- **Date range:** 1712 through the Spanish period
- **Where held:** **Louisiana Historical Center**, Old U.S. Mint, 400 Esplanade Ave., New Orleans — partially digitized; finding aids available
- **Online access:** Partial digitization at LHC; full access requires in-person visit or written request. **Digitization level: Partial / physical**
- **Per FamilySearch wiki ("Louisiana Genealogy, French Colonial Records"):** These records are foundational for colonial family reconstruction and should be consulted for any ancestor documented in the colony.

---

### Notarial archives

- **Significance:** New Orleans has the **oldest continuously operating notarial office in North America.** Under French (and Spanish) civil law, notaries recorded every major legal transaction.
- **Types:** Wills, marriage contracts, property sales, apprenticeships, manumissions, business agreements
- **Date range:** From the **1730s** onward
- **Where held:** **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center (NONAR)**, 1340 Poydras St., Suite 501, New Orleans — open to the public; some FHL microfilm
- **Online access:** Limited online finding aids; records themselves are **physical / microfilm** only. **Digitization level: Microfilm / physical**
- **Per FamilySearch wiki ("Louisiana Land and Property, Colonial Land Grants"):** Marriage contracts are especially valuable — they name both families, list property brought into the marriage, and often identify witnesses who may be relatives.

---

### Colonial census rolls (*recensements*)

- **What they are:** Periodic household enumerations listing family members, servants, and enslaved persons with rough ages and sometimes origins. Known enumeration years include approximately 1699, 1708, 1721, 1722, 1724, 1726, 1731, 1737, 1741, 1745.
- **Where held:** **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)**, Aix-en-Provence, France (Series G¹); some copies/transcriptions at the Louisiana Historical Center
- **Online access:** ANOM portal (anom.culture.gouv.fr) — some rolls digitized but **browse-only** (no name index). **Digitization level: Browse-only images (ANOM) / physical**
- **Published transcriptions:** Charles Maduell's *Census Tables for the French Colony of Louisiana* is a key finding aid — check WorldCat or your library.

---

### Land records

- **System:** French civil law concessions — not the English common-law or rectangular survey systems used in other U.S. states.
- **Types:** Crown concessions, Company of the Indies grants, individual purchase contracts, mortgage records
- **Where held:**
  - **American State Papers: Public Lands** — colonial-era land claims confirmed after U.S. acquisition; digitized free at **Library of Congress** (loc.gov) and **HathiTrust** (**Indexed + images**)
  - **Louisiana Historical Center** — Superior Council land grant records (physical / partial digitization)
  - **ANOM** — original French administrative grants (physical / browse-only)
  - **Louisiana State Land Office** — post-1803 land records and some colonial confirmations

---

### Probate records

- **Colonial period:** Estate matters handled by the **Superior Council** (see above).
- **1756 onward:** FamilySearch collection **"Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984"** (Collection ID 2078341) — ~456,789 indexed records, 228,394 images, beginning in the final years of French rule. **Digitization level: Indexed + images** (free on FamilySearch).
- **Note:** Under French civil law, estates were distributed among heirs rather than following primogeniture. Estate inventories routinely name spouses, children, and creditors — an excellent source for family reconstruction.

---

### Military records

- **Relevant conflicts:** Natchez War (1729–31), Chickasaw Wars (1730s–40s), Seven Years' War (1754–63)
- **Troops:** Primarily the **Compagnies franches de la Marine** (colonial marine infantry)
- **Record types:** Muster rolls, service records, officer commissions
- **Where held:** **ANOM**, Aix-en-Provence, Series D²C; some records at **Service Historique de la Défense (SHD)**, Vincennes, France
- **Online access:** ANOM portal — some digitized as browse-only images. **Digitization level: Browse-only images (ANOM) / physical**

---

### Immigration and passenger records

- **Context:** Colonists arrived primarily from France; German immigrants settled the Côte des Allemands from the 1720s; enslaved Africans arrived via the slave trade (primarily from Senegambia and the Bight of Benin during the French period).
- **Types:** Ship passenger lists (*rôles de passagers*), embarkation registers, slave ship manifests, engagé (indentured worker) contracts
- **Where held:** **ANOM** (Aix-en-Provence) holds the major embarkation and passenger records for French colonial Louisiana
- **Online access:** ANOM portal for some records (browse-only). **Digitization level: Browse-only images (ANOM) / physical**
- **Published transcriptions:** Several scholars have compiled colonist arrival lists. Key works: Winston De Ville's *Louisiana Colonials*; Glenn Conrad's *The German Coast*.

---

### Tax records

- Formal property tax rolls as known in later U.S. jurisdictions did not exist in this form during the French colonial period. The **colonial census rolls** (listed above) recorded property, livestock, and enslaved persons and serve as the functional substitute for this period.

---

### Cemetery records

- **Major burial sites:** Parish churchyards throughout the colony; St. Louis Cemetery No. 1 (New Orleans, established 1789 — Spanish period).
- **Online coverage:** FindAGrave.com has some coverage, but pre-1780 colonial inscriptions are sparse due to deterioration of above-ground tombs and wooden markers.
- **Primary source:** Parish burial registers (see Church Records) are the main documentary evidence for deaths in this period.

---

### Newspaper records

- **Not applicable.** The first newspaper in Louisiana — the *Moniteur de la Louisiane* — was not established until 1794, well after the French colonial period ended. No newspapers were published in French Louisiana (1699–1762).

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collections for this place | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Church Records 1718–1925 (ID 1612905, ~987K records); Probate Records 1756–1984 (ID 2078341, ~456K records) | Free |
| **ANOM portal** (anom.culture.gouv.fr) | Administrative correspondence, census rolls, military records, passenger lists — browse-only | Free |
| **Library of Congress / HathiTrust** | American State Papers: Public Lands (colonial land claims) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | Some Louisiana church and civil records (overlap with later period) | Subscription |
| **FamilySearch Catalog** (catalog.familysearch.org) | Microfilm of parish registers, Superior Council records, notarial records | Free catalog; film via FHL or FamilySearch center |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access method |
|---|---|---|
| **Louisiana Historical Center** (Old U.S. Mint, 400 Esplanade Ave., New Orleans) | Superior Council records, colonial court and land records | In-person; contact for research requests |
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center (NONAR)** (1340 Poydras St., Ste. 501) | Notarial acts from 1730s — marriage contracts, wills, property transactions | In-person; open to public |
| **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives** | Original parish registers for Catholic churches in the New Orleans area | In-person; written request |
| **Louisiana State Archives** (Baton Rouge) | State-level records (primarily post-1803); some colonial holdings | In-person / some online finding aids |
| **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)** (Aix-en-Provence, France) | Administrative correspondence, census rolls, military records, passenger lists | In-person or remote document requests; partial portal access |
| **Archivo General de Indias** (Seville, Spain) | Spanish colonial records post-1769 | PARES portal (pares.mcu.es) for some digitized records; in-person |
| **Family History Library (FHL)** (Salt Lake City) | Microfilm of Louisiana church records, Superior Council records, notarial records | In-person or loan via FamilySearch centers |
| **Tulane University — Louisiana Research Collection** | Manuscript collections, colonial documents, published transcriptions | In-person |
| **New Orleans Public Library — Louisiana Division** | Local history, published indexes and transcriptions | In-person |

---

## Records NOT online

These record types exist for the French colonial period but are **not** in any fully searchable online database:

- **Colonial census rolls (recensements)** — browse-only images at ANOM; no name index
- **Superior Council civil and criminal proceedings** — partial digitization at LHC; no comprehensive online index
- **Notarial acts (1730s–1769)** — physical at NONAR; some FHL microfilm
- **French military records (Compagnies franches de la Marine)** — ANOM, browse-only or physical
- **Passenger and embarkation lists** — ANOM, primarily physical or browse-only
- **Outlying parish registers** (settlements outside New Orleans) — some on FHL microfilm; many not digitized or indexed

---

## Known record losses

- **Founding years (1699–1718):** Records from the Biloxi/Mobile phase are sparse; administrative documents from this period are in French metropolitan archives rather than Louisiana.
- **New Orleans fires (1788 and 1794):** Two catastrophic fires during the early Spanish period destroyed much of the city; some notarial and administrative records were lost, though the notarial archives survived better than residential districts.
- **Climate and storage:** Louisiana's humidity has caused ongoing deterioration; some early registers are illegible or fragmentary.
- **Substitute sources when records are missing:**
  - Cross-reference registers from adjacent parishes
  - Notarial acts often duplicate vital information from church records
  - Superior Council estate inventories name heirs and family relationships
  - Colonial census rolls place households at specific locations and dates

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with parish registers — always.** There is no civil registration for this period. The Catholic Church substituted entirely for the civil vital registration system.

2. **French-language skills are essential.** All colonial records are in French (with Latin in church records). Consider hiring a professional with French paleography expertise, or use FamilySearch's French handwriting guides.

3. **Use the Code Noir as a genealogical tool.** The 1724 Code Noir mandated Catholic baptism of enslaved persons. Parish registers recording these baptisms are often the only documentary evidence for enslaved ancestors in this era — note the enslaver named as godparent or owner, and follow that household through subsequent records.

4. **Cross the French/Spanish colonial line.** If your ancestor lived through the 1760s–1780s, they appear in records under two governments. The Spanish notarial system continued French record-keeping conventions, and families span both periods seamlessly.

5. **Consult published transcriptions before traveling.** Scholars have transcribed and indexed many colonial Louisiana records. Locate these via WorldCat: Winston De Ville's *Louisiana Colonials* series; Charles Maduell's *Census Tables for the French Colony of Louisiana*; Stanley Clisby Arthur & George de Kernion's *Old Families of Louisiana*.

6. **The ANOM portal is free.** For records held in France, browse anom.culture.gouv.fr. Key series: **C¹³** (Louisiana administrative correspondence); **G¹** (census and statistical records); **D²C** (colonial military personnel).

7. **Check the FamilySearch Catalog** (catalog.familysearch.org) under "Louisiana" for microfilm holdings of parish registers, Superior Council records, and notarial acts — many items on film have not yet been digitized.

8. **Key FamilySearch wiki articles** to read:
   - *Louisiana Genealogy* → section "French Colonial Records (1699–1762)"
   - *Louisiana Land and Property* → section "Colonial Land Grants"

9. **Know your ancestor's specific locale.** Colonial Louisiana was not divided into formal civil parishes (that came with U.S. statehood). Records are organized by Catholic parish or administrative district: New Orleans, Pointe Coupée, Natchitoches, Opelousas, Attakapas, and Illinois Country each have distinct record sets. Identifying where your ancestor lived focuses the search considerably.

---

*Guide saved to `louisiana-french-colonial-locality-guide.md` in your working folder. Sources: FamilySearch wiki ("Louisiana Genealogy," "Louisiana Land and Property"); Wikipedia ("French Louisiana"); FamilySearch collections_search (Louisiana, United States); Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer portal documentation.*

# Locality Guide: Pennsylvania Coal Mining Towns (Late 1800s — c. 1860–1910)

## Jurisdiction overview

This guide covers two distinct coal zones:

**Anthracite region (northeastern PA):** Schuylkill (formed 1811), Luzerne, Lackawanna, Carbon, and Northumberland counties — the core of what the Wikipedia article "Coal Region" calls the nation's leading coal-producing zone. County seats: Pottsville (Schuylkill), Wilkes-Barre (Luzerne), Scranton (Lackawanna), Jim Thorpe/Mauch Chunk (Carbon).

**Bituminous region (western PA):** Westmoreland, Fayette, Allegheny, and Washington counties.

- **Economy:** Coal mining, railroads, iron and steel. Major employers included the Philadelphia & Reading Coal and Iron Company, Lehigh Valley Coal, and the Pennsylvania Railroad's coal subsidiaries.
- **Ethnic composition:** Irish and Welsh (earliest waves, 1820s–1850s); German (1840s–1860s); then a large second immigration wave from Poland, Lithuania, Slovakia, Ukraine, and Italy (1880s–1900s). Ethnic enclaves were tightly defined — whole mining patches were often dominated by one nationality.
- **Religious denominations:** Roman Catholic (with separate nationality parishes — Irish, Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Italian each distinct); Welsh Congregational and Baptist; Methodist Episcopal; German Lutheran; Eastern Rite Catholic (Ukrainian, Carpatho-Rusyn).
- **Key events generating records:** Avondale Mine disaster, Luzerne County (1869 — 110 killed; led to Pennsylvania's first mine safety legislation); Molly Maguires trials, Schuylkill County (1875–77); Long Strike of 1875; formation of the UMWA (1890); Lattimer Massacre, Luzerne County (1897).

---

## Boundary changes — the critical one

**Lackawanna County was carved from Luzerne County in 1878.** This is the single most consequential boundary change for researchers in this region. Records for Scranton and surrounding townships **before 1878** are in **Luzerne County**; after 1878 they are in **Lackawanna County**. Searching the wrong county is the most common mistake.

- Carbon County was formed from Northampton and Monroe counties in 1843. Earlier records for the Mauch Chunk area are in Northampton County.
- Schuylkill County formed from Berks and Northampton in 1811.
- Other coal counties had stable boundaries throughout the late 1800s.

---

## Vital records (civil registration)

⚠️ **Pennsylvania did NOT require statewide registration of births and deaths until 1906.** For the entire late-1800s period, there is no statewide civil birth or death record. Church records are the primary substitute.

**What exists:**
- **Marriage licenses/bonds:** Recorded at the county level throughout this period at the county courthouse. Several FamilySearch collections cover these:
  - *Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950* — **524,189 records**, 1,048,378 persons, 312,045 images. **Indexed + images.** (Collection 1921317)
  - *Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950* — **312,045 records**, 624,090 persons, 156,023 images. **Indexed + images.** (Collection 1571957)
  - *Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900* — **198,765 records**, index only, no images. **Index only.** (Collection 1502909)
- **Death certificates begin 1906:** The *Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968* collection on FamilySearch has **4,218,763 fully indexed records with images** (Collection 2255317) — just outside the core window, but vital for people born in the late 1800s who died after 1906.
- **Gaps:** No statewide birth or death certificates exist for 1860–1905. Church registers and mine inspector reports are the substitutes.

---

## Church records — the most important record type

Because civil registration did not exist, the baptism register is often the only birth record; the burial register the only death record for this entire period.

- **Catholic parishes (Irish, Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Italian, Ukrainian):** Registers of baptisms, marriages, and burials in Latin and/or the vernacular. Critically, each national group typically had its *own* parish in a coal town — an Irish and a Polish parish might be a block apart, each keeping separate registers in different languages. Contact the **Diocese of Scranton** (Lackawanna, Luzerne, Carbon, and surrounding counties) and the **Diocese of Allentown** (Schuylkill and Carbon counties).
- **Welsh Congregational and Baptist:** Welsh chapel registers are sometimes at local churches, the **Presbyterian Historical Society** (Philadelphia), or FamilySearch microfilm.
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* — **1,245,830 records**, 982,415 persons, 607,220 images. **Indexed + images.** (Collection 1401638) This aggregated statewide collection is the best first stop for any pre-1906 vital event.
- **Language note:** Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, and Ukrainian Catholic registers from the 1880s–1900s may be in the immigrant language or Latin. Translation assistance may be needed.

---

## Census records

| Census | Notes | Access |
|---|---|---|
| 1860, 1870, 1880, 1900, 1910 | All list occupation ("miner," "mine laborer," etc.). 1880 adds household relationship | Indexed + images — FamilySearch, Ancestry |
| **1890** | Largely destroyed by fire (1921 Washington, D.C.) | Use substitutes below |
| **Mortality schedules** 1850–1880 | List every death in the 12 months before the census — name, age, birthplace, occupation, cause of death. Mine accidents appear here. Critical in the absence of death certificates | Indexed + images — FamilySearch, Ancestry |
| **1890 Veterans Schedule** | Survived the 1890 fire; lists Union veterans with service details | Indexed + images — Ancestry |

Pennsylvania had no separate state census in the late 1800s.

---

## Mining and occupational records (unique to this region)

**Search these before many standard sources** — they are the most distinctive record type for this area and period.

| Record | Coverage | FamilySearch digitization level |
|---|---|---|
| Mine Worker Employment Cards | Schuylkill County, 1870–1900 — 410 images | **Indexed + images** (95% indexed, full-text searchable; imageGroup 002240015) |
| Mine Accident Reports | Luzerne County, 1870–1895 — 287 images | **Browse-only** (0% indexed; imageGroup 002240016) |
| Colliery Payroll Ledgers | Lackawanna County, 1878–1900 — 199 images | **Browse-only** (0% indexed; imageGroup 002240017) |
| **State Mine Inspector Reports** (annual from 1870) | Statewide — list every fatal mine accident with name, age, nationality, cause of death | PA State Archives; some years on HathiTrust/Google Books |
| Company employment records (payrolls, hiring ledgers) | Fragmentary — PA State Archives, Historical Society of Schuylkill County, Anthracite Heritage Museum | Physical only / microfilm |
| UMWA membership rolls (from 1890) | UMWA archives | Contact UMWA directly |

The **State Mine Inspector annual reports** (published from 1870) are a true death-record substitute for miners killed on the job — they predate civil death registration by 36 years.

---

## Probate and court records

- *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* on FamilySearch — **893,214 records**, 446,607 images. **Indexed + images.** (Collection 1417683)
- Originals at the **county Register of Wills**; may contain more detail than the index.
- **Naturalizations:** Pre-1906, could be filed in any court. Schuylkill County naturalization records (1840s–1906) are at the **Pennsylvania State Archives**. Luzerne and Lackawanna records are at the county courthouses, partly on FamilySearch microfilm. After September 1906, all naturalizations went through federal courts — indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch; physical records at **NARA Philadelphia**.
- **Coroner's inquests:** Mine accident deaths triggered inquests; records name the deceased, witnesses, and next of kin. Held at county archives. **Physical only** — underused but invaluable.

---

## Land records

- Pennsylvania is a **state-land state** (metes and bounds). Most coal miners rented company housing in patch towns rather than owning land, so deeds are less central than in farming areas — but tax lists and deed books still document presence and movement.
- Warrants, surveys, and patents: **Pennsylvania State Archives** (also searchable via the online PA Land Records database).
- County deed books: **county Recorder of Deeds** — largely physical only or microfilm.

---

## Military records

- **Civil War (1861–1865):** Many Irish and Welsh miners served in Pennsylvania regiments.
  - Service records: NARA, indexed on **Fold3** and Ancestry. **Indexed + images.**
  - Pension files: NARA — extremely rich; may include affidavits from neighbors, marriage certificates, and medical histories. **Indexed + images** on Fold3/Ancestry.
  - Pennsylvania veterans' card file: **Pennsylvania State Archives.**
- **Spanish-American War (1898):** Service records at NARA.
- **WWI Draft (1917–18):** Falls just after this period, but draft cards for men born in the late 1800s are available — **indexed + images** on Ancestry.

---

## Cemetery records

- **FindAGrave** (findagrave.com): Extensive coverage for coal-region cemeteries; headstone photos contributed by volunteers. Free. **Indexed + images where photos exist.**
- **BillionGraves** (billiongraves.com): GPS-tagged headstone coverage. Free with registration.
- **Sexton records and burial registers:** Held at the cemetery office or church; survival varies. Some are in county historical society collections.

---

## Newspaper records

Local newspapers are rich genealogical sources — death notices, obituaries, mine accident reports, court proceedings, and immigrant community news.

- **Key papers:** *Miners' Journal* (Pottsville, from 1825 — the principal anthracite paper); *Scranton Republican*; *Scranton Times*; *Wilkes-Barre Record*; ethnic-language papers (Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak) from the 1880s onward.
- **Newspapers.com:** Extensive Pennsylvania holdings including the *Miners' Journal*; full-text searchable. Subscription. **Indexed + images.**
- **Chronicling America** (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov): Select coal-region papers; free.
- **Historical Society of Schuylkill County:** Microfilm runs of the *Miners' Journal*.
- **Pennsylvania State Library:** Microfilm of local papers statewide.

---

## Immigration and emigration records

- **Primary ports:** New York (Castle Garden through 1890; Ellis Island from 1892) and Philadelphia.
- **Passenger lists:**
  - Pre-1891: Customs lists (sparse data) — FamilySearch, Ancestry. **Indexed + images.**
  - 1891 onward: Immigration manifests (detailed — birthplace, last residence, destination, U.S. contact) — Ancestry, FamilySearch, **Ellis Island database** (libertyellisfoundation.org, free for 1892–1957). **Indexed + images.**
  - ⚠️ Always examine the **full manifest page** — family members, co-workers from the same village, and chain-migration contacts are often listed nearby. Children including infants are listed.
- **Naturalization:** See Probate and Court Records above.

---

## Tax records

County tax assessment lists (tax duplicates) survive from the early 1800s and provide annual snapshots of who lived in each township. Key substitute for the missing 1890 census. Available on FamilySearch microfilm for many counties, or at the Pennsylvania State Archives. **Largely microfilm or physical only.**

---

## Other record types

- **Fraternal and mutual aid society records:** AOH (Irish), Welsh St. David's societies, Polish National Alliance, Lithuanian Alliance, Slovak Jednota — membership records, death benefit payments. Held by the organizations or at the **Historical Society of Pennsylvania** (Philadelphia, which holds the Balch Institute ethnic collections). Survival is uneven.
- **City directories:** Published annually for Pottsville, Scranton, and Wilkes-Barre from the 1860s–1870s onward. Available on Ancestry and at the Pennsylvania State Library. Excellent for locating a family between census years.
- **School enrollment registers:** At county historical societies and the Pennsylvania State Archives. **Physical only.**

---

## Repository guide

### Online

| Repository | Key holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Church & Town Records 1708–1985; County Marriages 1885–1950; Probate Records 1683–1994; Death Certificates 1906–1968; Mine Worker Employment Cards (Schuylkill); Mine Accident Reports (Luzerne, browse-only); Colliery Payrolls (Lackawanna, browse-only); Federal Census 1860–1910 | Free |
| **Ancestry** (ancestry.com) | Federal Census; passenger lists; naturalizations; Civil War service and pensions; city directories; newspapers | Subscription |
| **Fold3** (fold3.com) | Civil War service records; pension files | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | *Miners' Journal*; Scranton and Wilkes-Barre papers; full-text searchable | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** (loc.gov/collections/chronicling-america) | Select PA coal-region papers | Free |
| **FindAGrave** (findagrave.com) | Cemetery headstone photos and transcriptions | Free |
| **Ellis Island Database** (libertyellisfoundation.org) | NY arrivals 1892–1957 | Free |
| **PA State Archives Online** (digitalarchives.state.pa.us) | Land records; naturalization indexes; mine records | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Key holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) | Land records; naturalization records; mine inspector reports; military records | In-person; some by mail |
| **Historical Society of Schuylkill County** (Pottsville) | *Miners' Journal* microfilm; mining company records; genealogical files | In-person |
| **Anthracite Heritage Museum** (Scranton) | Mining community records; ethnic materials; employment/accident records | In-person |
| **Lackawanna County Historical Society** (Scranton) | County records; local newspapers; genealogical collections | In-person |
| **Luzerne County Historical Society** (Wilkes-Barre) | County records; newspapers | In-person |
| **Diocese of Scranton Archives** | Catholic parish registers for Lackawanna, Luzerne, Carbon, and surrounding counties | Contact archives |
| **Diocese of Allentown Archives** | Catholic parish registers for Schuylkill and Carbon counties | Contact archives |
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania** (Philadelphia) | Balch Institute ethnic collections (Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Ukrainian fraternal records) | In-person |
| **NARA Philadelphia — Mid-Atlantic Region** | Post-1906 naturalizations; federal court records | In-person; some via Ancestry |
| **Pennsylvania State Library** (Harrisburg) | Newspaper microfilm; city directories; Mine Inspector report runs | In-person |
| **County courthouses** (each county) | Deed books; probate files; marriage licenses; pre-1906 naturalizations; coroner's inquests | In-person or mail |

---

## Records NOT online

These exist but are absent from online databases — do not mistake online absence for nonexistence:

- Coroner's inquest records (most counties) — county archives, physical only
- Most colliery payroll and hiring records — county historical societies and PA State Archives; the FamilySearch browse-only volumes for Lackawanna are a partial exception
- Fraternal and mutual aid society records (AOH, Polish National Alliance, Lithuanian Alliance, Slovak Jednota, etc.)
- Individual Catholic parish registers not captured in the FamilySearch aggregated collection
- County tax assessment lists for most coal counties — microfilm only
- Hospital records — held by successor institutions
- School enrollment registers — county historical societies and PA State Archives
- UMWA membership rolls — contact UMWA directly

---

## Known record losses

- **1890 Federal Census:** Destroyed by fire in Washington, D.C. (1921). Substitutes: 1890 Veterans Schedule (survived), state mine inspector reports, tax assessment lists, city directories, church registers.
- **No major courthouse fires** for the principal anthracite counties during this period — Schuylkill, Luzerne, Lackawanna, and Carbon courthouses survived intact. This is favorable compared to many states.
- **Fragmentary company records:** Mining companies that changed hands or dissolved may have discarded employment records; do not assume a company record survives without verifying with the holding repository.

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with the FamilySearch church collection.** *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* (Collection 1401638) is the best first stop for any pre-1906 vital event.

2. **Remember the 1878 Lackawanna split.** Records for the Scranton area before 1878 are in Luzerne County. Searching the wrong county is the most common mistake for this region.

3. **Use Mine Inspector reports as a death-record substitute.** Published annually from 1870, they name every miner killed in Pennsylvania. Check HathiTrust and Google Books; the full run is at the Pennsylvania State Archives. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Pennsylvania Occupational Records — Coal Mining.")*

4. **Identify the specific ethnic parish.** In coal towns, Irish, Polish, Slovak, Lithuanian, and Ukrainian Catholics attended separate parishes that kept separate registers in separate languages. Knowing your ancestor's nationality is essential to finding the right register.

5. **Read the full manifest page, not just your ancestor's line.** Chain migration was the norm. Co-workers from the same village and family members — including young children and infants — are listed nearby on immigrant passenger manifests.

6. **Coroner's inquests are underused.** For any ancestor who died in a mine accident, a coroner's inquest was typically held and filed at the county courthouse. These records name witnesses, identify next of kin, and sometimes provide birthplace.

7. **City directories bridge census gaps.** Published annually for Pottsville, Scranton, and Wilkes-Barre, they track where a family lived and worked between the 10-year federal enumerations — especially important given the destroyed 1890 census.

8. **Explore FAN cluster research.** Miners from the same village in Ireland, Wales, Poland, or Slovakia emigrated together and settled in the same patch. Researching neighbors and associates often breaks brick walls by revealing relatives who arrived under variant names or different dates.

9. **Consult the FamilySearch Wiki.** The article "Pennsylvania Occupational Records" (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Pennsylvania_Occupational_Records) provides detailed guidance on company and union records specific to coal mining. The article "Pennsylvania Vital Records" (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Pennsylvania_Vital_Records) covers the pre-1906 registration gap in depth.

10. **For ethnic fraternal records**, the **Historical Society of Pennsylvania** (Philadelphia) holds the Balch Institute ethnic collections covering Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, and Ukrainian immigrant organizations active in the coal region.

---

*Sources: FamilySearch Wiki ("Pennsylvania Occupational Records," "Pennsylvania Vital Records," "Pennsylvania Land Records"); Wikipedia ("Coal Region," "Coal mining in Pennsylvania"); FamilySearch collections search and volume search results.*

---

# Locality Guide: Massachusetts — Early Puritan Settlements (1620–1700)

## Jurisdiction overview

The first thing to understand about this period is that **two separate colonies** operated here simultaneously, each with its own government and record system:

- **Plymouth Colony (1620–1691)** — founded by the Separatist Pilgrims aboard the Mayflower; administered as a single unit until it formed its own counties (Plymouth, Barnstable, Bristol) in 1685.
- **Massachusetts Bay Colony (1628–1691)** — Puritan-dominated; chartered in England and settled in mass during the **Great Migration (~20,000 settlers, 1630–1640)**. Created the first four counties in 1636: Suffolk, Essex, Middlesex, and Norfolk.

The two merged in **1691** into the Province of Massachusetts Bay under a royal charter. Always identify which colony governed your ancestor's town — it determines which record series applies.

Population: ~900 at Plymouth (1630) → ~20,000 new arrivals by 1640 → ~56,000–70,000 English colonists by 1700.

---

## Boundary changes affecting research

| Date | Event | Research implication |
|------|--------|----------------------|
| 1620 | Plymouth Colony founded — no county structure | Records are colony-level (court/governor) |
| 1636 | Bay Colony creates Suffolk, Essex, Middlesex, Norfolk counties | County-level land, probate, and court records begin |
| 1685 | Plymouth Colony forms Plymouth, Barnstable, Bristol counties | Earlier Plymouth records remain colony-level |
| 1691 | The two colonies merge into Province of Massachusetts Bay | Plymouth county records absorbed; single provincial system |

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (town-level civil registration)
- **Start date: 1639** — Massachusetts Bay Colony mandated town recording of births, marriages, and deaths, one of the earliest civil registration mandates in North America.
- **What exists:** Births, marriages, and deaths in town clerk books for each incorporated town.
- **Where held:** Original books with each town clerk or transferred to county/state archives. Published abstracts for 200+ towns in the *Vital Records of [Town Name] to 1850* series (the Tanner–Corbin series) — available at NEHGS, major libraries, and on FamilySearch/Ancestry.
- **FamilySearch:** *Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890* (Collection ID 2061550) — ~3.4 million indexed records with images. ✅ **Indexed + images.**
- **Gaps:** Towns founded after 1639 have later starts; record quality varied by town clerk; the 1639–1649 decade tends to be sparse.

### Church records — **CRITICAL for this period**
- **Denominations:** Congregationalism was the established church and the community backbone. Also: Separatist (Plymouth), Baptist, Quakers (persecuted until 1680s), small Anglican presence.
- **Record types:** Membership admissions, baptisms, marriages, disciplinary proceedings (excommunications), burial registers.
- **Where held:** Congregational Library & Archives (Boston); NEHGS; Massachusetts Archives; many published in *Colonial Society of Massachusetts* series.
- **FamilySearch:** *Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920* (Collection ID 1921657) — ~988,000 indexed records with images. ✅ **Indexed + images.**
- **Research note:** Always search church records AND town vital records — neither is complete on its own. Many baptisms appear only in the church register; many marriages only in the town book.

### Colonial enumerations (no federal census before 1790)
There is no census for this period. The best substitutes:

| Source | Date | Coverage |
|--------|------|----------|
| 1623 Division of Land (Plymouth) | 1623 | Heads of household |
| 1627 Division of Cattle (Plymouth) | 1627 | Heads of household + family members |
| 1643 List of Males Able to Bear Arms (Plymouth) | 1643 | Adult males by town |
| Colony and town tax/rate lists | 1640s onward | Adult male property owners by town |

Tax rate lists at the Massachusetts Archives are the primary substitute for census records throughout the 1600s.

### Probate and court records
- **Date range:** Bay Colony probate from 1636 (county courts); Plymouth Colony probate from the 1620s (colony court).
- **Types:** Wills, inventories, letters of administration, guardianship bonds. Inventories are especially valuable — they list every household item and typically name heirs.
- **Where held:** County probate courts; Massachusetts Archives. Major published series: *Essex County Probate Records* (Essex Institute); *Plymouth Colony Records* (Shurtleff & Pulsifer, 12 vols.).
- **FamilySearch:** *Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991* (Collection ID 1678901) — ~568,000 indexed records with images. ✅ **Indexed + images.**
- **Court records:** General Court, county courts, and Courts of General Sessions document debt, civil disputes, criminal cases, and guardianships — often naming family members not found anywhere else. Published for Essex County (9 volumes) and in *Plymouth Colony Records*.

### Land records
- **System:** Metes and bounds. Town proprietors made original grants; county registries of deeds recorded transfers.
- **Date range:** Bay Colony deeds from the 1630s; Plymouth Colony from the 1620s.
- **Where held:** County Registries of Deeds — among the best-preserved colonial records in America. Suffolk County from 1640; Essex County from 1638; Plymouth County from the 1680s (earlier in *Plymouth Colony Records*).
- **Town proprietor records:** Separate from deed books; record original lot allotments. Held at individual town archives or published in local town histories — **no central online index exists**.
- **Access level:** Most pre-1700 deed volumes are ⚠️ **browse-only images** on FamilySearch; partial online indexes exist for some counties.

### Military records
- **Relevant conflicts:**
  - Pequot War (1636–1638): Militia musters at Massachusetts Archives.
  - **King Philip's War (1675–1676):** Muster rolls, captain's lists, war damages claims — at the Massachusetts Archives; partially published in George Madison Bodge's *Soldiers in King Philip's War* (1906).
- **Access level:** Mostly 📋 **physical only / microfilm**. Published compilations at NEHGS and major research libraries.
- **Note:** No pension or bounty land records exist for this period — those systems began after the Revolution.

### Cemetery records
- **Oldest surviving burial grounds:** Burial Hill, Plymouth (1620s); King's Chapel Burying Ground, Boston (1630); Granary Burying Ground, Boston (1660); Charter Street Cemetery, Salem (1637).
- **Headstone survival:** Unusually good for the 1600s — many slate markers survive in New England.
- **Online:** FindAGrave and BillionGraves have extensive transcriptions and photos of 17th-century markers. ✅ **Indexed + images (free).**
- **Published transcriptions:** DAR and local historical societies have transcribed hundreds of Massachusetts burying grounds; volumes at NEHGS.

### Passenger lists and immigration records
- **Context:** Virtually all 17th-century Massachusetts settlers arrived from England. No comprehensive arrival passenger lists were kept in New England ports.
- **Primary sources for 1620s–1640s arrivals:**
  - English port departure records (1637 Port Book)
  - English parish certificates of good standing (in English church archives)
  - William Bradford's *Of Plymouth Plantation* (names Plymouth settlers)
  - Published reconstructions: Charles Edward Banks's *Planters of the Commonwealth*; *The Winthrop Fleet*
  - **Robert Charles Anderson's *The Great Migration* series (NEHGS)** — the most comprehensive modern reconstruction, documenting every known settler 1620–1643 with full citations
  - *Silver Books* (General Society of Mayflower Descendants) — five-generation lineages for Mayflower passengers
- ⚠️ **Important:** The FamilySearch collection *Massachusetts, Passenger Lists* (Collection ID 1784312) covers **1820–1891 only** — it does not apply to the 1600s. For 17th-century arrivals, use the Great Migration study and published compilations.
- **Access level:** Published compilations (physical or at AmericanAncestors.org/Ancestry) — **no comprehensive indexed online database for 17th-century arrivals**.

### Tax records
- **Date range:** Bay Colony rate lists from the early 1640s onward.
- **Where held:** Massachusetts Archives (colony-level); town archives (local lists).
- **Substitute value:** ⭐ Essential — these are your primary substitute for census records in the 1600s. Adult male taxpayers are listed by town; a rate list can confirm a person's presence in a specific year.
- **Access level:** 📋 Mostly **physical only** or microfilm. Published extracts for some towns.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collections for this place | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Town Vital Records 1620–1890 (3.4M, ID 2061550); Church Records 1630–1920 (988K, ID 1921657); Probate Records 1635–1991 (568K, ID 1678901) | Free |
| **AmericanAncestors.org** (NEHGS) | Great Migration series; MA vital records index; manuscript collections | Subscription |
| **Ancestry.com** | *Vital Records of MA Towns to 1850*; early colonial compilations; published genealogies | Subscription |
| **Internet Archive / HathiTrust** | Full text of *Plymouth Colony Records*, Essex Court Records, Bradford's *Of Plymouth Plantation*, and many published 19th-century compilations | Free |
| **Massachusetts Historical Society** (masshist.org) | Winthrop Papers and many colonial manuscripts digitized | Free |
| **FindAGrave** | MA cemetery transcriptions including 17th-century burial grounds | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Contact |
|---|---|---|
| **Massachusetts Archives** (Boston) | Original colony records from 1620s; General Court records; tax lists; militia rolls; land records | archives.lib.state.ma.us |
| **NEHGS** (Boston) | Largest genealogical library in New England; Great Migration files; manuscript collections | americanancestors.org |
| **Massachusetts Historical Society** (Boston) | Winthrop Papers; colonial manuscripts; maps | masshist.org |
| **Congregational Library & Archives** (Boston) | Church records for Congregational meetings statewide | congregationallibrary.org |
| **County Registries of Deeds** (Suffolk, Essex, Middlesex, Plymouth) | Original deed books from 1630s–1640s | In-person; some online indexes |
| **County Probate Courts** (same counties) | Original probate volumes from 1635 | In-person; microfilm at FHL |
| **Pilgrim Hall Museum** (Plymouth) | Mayflower manuscripts; Plymouth Colony materials | pilgrimhall.org |
| **Family History Library** (Salt Lake City) | Microfilm of most MA county records, church records, probate, deeds | familysearch.org (affiliate libraries) |

---

## Records NOT online

These record types **exist** but have no comprehensive online indexed database:

- **Town proprietor records** (original land division records) — at individual town archives or in local history publications; no central index
- **Tax and rate lists (1640s–1690s)** — Massachusetts Archives and town archives; scattered published transcripts only
- **Pre-King Philip's War militia rolls** — Massachusetts Archives; partially published, not indexed online
- **Middlesex and Suffolk county court records (1600s)** — originals at Massachusetts Archives; outside the published Essex and Plymouth series, largely unindexed
- **Town meeting minutes (1630s–1690s)** — each town's original record books; held by town clerk or deposited at county/state archives; availability varies
- **Quaker meeting records** — held by New England Yearly Meeting Archives, Providence, RI; not on FamilySearch

---

## Known record losses

| Loss | Date | What was destroyed | Substitute sources |
|------|------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **King Philip's War** | 1675–1676 | Town record books for dozens of frontier towns: Deerfield, Northampton, Brookfield, Medfield, Mendon, and others along the Connecticut River valley | Colony-level court records; church records; neighboring town records; published compilations |
| **Individual town records** | Various | Some early town clerk books lost to fire, flood, or neglect before the 19th century | *Vital Records to 1850* series notes gaps; probate and court records as substitutes |
| **No statewide duplicate registration** | — | Because records were town-level only (no colony-wide copy filed), any town book loss is permanent unless transcripts existed | Published series; church records; probate |

**If your ancestor lived in western or central Massachusetts before 1680**, assume records may be missing and plan to triangulate using probate, court, church, and neighboring-town records.

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with the *Great Migration* series.** Robert Charles Anderson's NEHGS volumes cover every known settler 1620–1643 with full source citations. If your ancestor arrived before 1643, this is your first stop. Access via AmericanAncestors.org (subscription) or in print.

2. **Two colonies = two record systems.** Know whether your ancestor lived under Plymouth Colony or Massachusetts Bay Colony — it determines which records to search and where they are held.

3. **Cross-check town vital records AND church records.** Neither is complete. Many baptisms appear only in the church register; many marriages appear only in the town book. Always search both.

4. **Court records reveal family structure.** Debt cases, estate disputes, and guardianship proceedings routinely name spouses, children, siblings, and neighbors. The published Essex County court series (9 volumes) and *Plymouth Colony Records* are well indexed.

5. **Use tax lists as a census substitute.** Colony and town rate lists are the only way to confirm a person's presence in a town in a specific year during the 1600s.

6. **Then trace the English origin.** Most Bay Colony Puritans emigrated from East Anglia (Suffolk, Norfolk, Essex counties) and the East Midlands. English parish registers, bishop's transcripts, and the 1637 port departure records are the next step once you've established the immigrant ancestor.

7. **King Philip's War as a research wall.** If a family disappears from records around 1675–1676, consider war-related displacement. Check neighboring towns and Connecticut for refugees.

8. **Watch for naming patterns.** New England Puritans frequently reused names across generations — naming children after grandparents and Biblical figures. Use probate inventories (which name minor children) to distinguish individuals of the same name.

9. **Many primary sources are free online.** *Plymouth Colony Records*, Bradford's *Of Plymouth Plantation*, the Essex Court series, and the Winthrop Papers are all freely available on Internet Archive or HathiTrust — often with better indexes than working from microfilm.

---

The guide has been saved to **`massachusetts-early-colonial-locality-guide.md`** in your working folder.

---

**Bottom line for researchers:** Early Massachusetts is exceptional by colonial American standards — town-level vital registration began in 1639, probate and deeds survive from the 1630s, and an unparalleled corpus of published indexed compilations exists (especially the *Great Migration* study). The two main challenges are (1) the split Plymouth/Bay Colony record system before 1691, and (2) King Philip's War losses in frontier towns. For any family that arrived before 1643, the *Great Migration* series at NEHGS is the single most efficient starting point.

# Locality Guide: Oklahoma Territory — Land Run Era (1889–1907)

## Jurisdiction overview

Oklahoma Territory did not exist as an organized entity until the **Organic Act of May 2, 1890**, which was itself a direct consequence of the Land Run of April 22, 1889. Before that date, the "Unassigned Lands" had no county government at all. Records therefore begin in mid-1890 at the earliest — and no statewide vital registration existed until *after* statehood (1907).

A second critical fact: **two parallel jurisdictions** covered the area of present-day Oklahoma simultaneously.

| Territory | Geography | Record system |
|---|---|---|
| **Oklahoma Territory** | West and central Oklahoma | Federal GLO, territorial district courts, county records |
| **Indian Territory** | East Oklahoma | Five Civilized Tribes, BIA, Dawes Commission, tribal courts |

Always determine which territory your ancestor lived in before searching — the records are entirely different.

- **Capital:** Guthrie (Oklahoma Territory, 1890–1907)
- **Population:** ~50,000 Land Run participants on Day 1 (April 22, 1889); ~61,800 at the 1890 territorial census; ~790,000 combined territories by 1900; ~1.4 million at statehood
- **Settlers came from:** Primarily Kansas, Texas, Missouri, and Arkansas — searching *origin-state* records is essential
- **Statehood:** November 16, 1907 (both territories merged into the State of Oklahoma)

---

## Boundary changes

- **Pre-May 1890:** No organized counties. The only federal presence was the land offices at Guthrie and Kingfisher.
- **1890–1907:** Oklahoma Territory counties created in waves after each land run (1889, 1891, 1892, 1893 Cherokee Outlet, 1895). A family's county may not have existed at the date of your target event — look to the parent land office or parent county.
- **Indian Territory:** Remained under tribal governance (Cherokee, Choctaw, Chickasaw, Creek/Muscogee, Seminole) throughout the period, with a parallel U.S. federal court system. Not part of Oklahoma Territory.
- **1907:** Both territories merged at statehood. Post-1907 records are held under state authority; pre-1907 records remain split between the two systems.

---

## Available record types

### ⭐ Land records — the anchor record for Land Run research

**This is where to start for any family that participated in a land run.**

- **What exists:** Homestead entry files — application, proof of settlement with witness testimony, and final patent. Rejected and cancelled entries also survive and contain family details. Town-lot records document urban settlers in the new territorial towns.
- **Date range:** April 22, 1889 onward
- **What a file contains:** Claimant's name, residence, physical description, date of settlement, names of two or more witnesses (neighbors or relatives — a direct FAN-cluster lead), testimony about improvements made, wife and children often named
- **Where held:**
  - **BLM General Land Office Records** ([glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov)) — search patents by name; many images free online — **Indexed + images**
  - **FamilySearch** — *Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907* (collection 1541872; 234,567 records, 117,283 images) — **Indexed + images**
  - **National Archives, Washington D.C.** — Record Group 49, General Land Office; full case files (request through NARA catalog) — **Physical**
  - **Oklahoma Historical Society** — town-lot records, supplementary territorial land files

> **Don't skip rejected claims.** "Sooner" contestees and settlers who could not prove up had their claims cancelled, but those files at NARA still contain family information.

---

### Census records

| Census | Coverage | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1890 Oklahoma Territory special census** | Oklahoma Territory counties only | **Browse-only / partial index** | One of the very few surviving 1890 enumerations in the nation — the main federal 1890 census was largely destroyed in 1921. Held at the Oklahoma Historical Society. |
| **1900 federal census** | Both Oklahoma Territory and Indian Territory (enumerated separately) | **Indexed + images** | FamilySearch, Ancestry |
| **1910 federal census** | State of Oklahoma | **Indexed + images** | FamilySearch, Ancestry |
| **Tribal census rolls** | Indian Territory | **Indexed + images** | Five Civilized Tribes conducted own censuses through the 1890s; part of FamilySearch collection 1987432 |
| **School censuses** | Oklahoma Territory counties | **Physical only** | Oklahoma Historical Society; useful for identifying children not in other records |
| **County tax rolls** | Oklahoma Territory counties | **Physical / microfilm** | Oklahoma Historical Society; substitute for census years; annual |

---

### Vital records

**There are no territorial-era birth or death certificates.** Oklahoma Territory never enacted a civil registration law. Statewide registration began in October 1908, and compliance was poor until the 1930s. For 1889–1907, use substitutes:

- **Marriage records** — begin with county organization (1890); county courthouses are the primary holder. FamilySearch collection *Oklahoma, County Marriages, 1890–1995* (ID 2134789; 456,789 records, 228,394 images) — **Indexed + images**
- **Best substitutes for births/deaths:** Church registers · Homestead proof testimony (often names wife and children with ages) · Newspaper notices · Cemetery records · Probate filings

---

### Church records

- **Denominations:** Baptist, Methodist, Catholic, Presbyterian (especially in Indian Territory missions), Disciples of Christ, African Methodist Episcopal
- **Indian Territory mission churches** date to the 1820s–1830s and are among the oldest organized records in the entire region
- **FamilySearch** — *Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965* (ID 1654321; 123,456 records, 61,728 images) — **Indexed + images**
- **Oklahoma Conference, United Methodist Church Archives** — Methodist registers statewide
- **Diocese of Oklahoma City** — Catholic registers
- **Presbyterian Historical Society** (Philadelphia) — Indian Territory Presbyterian mission records, 1820s onward
- **American Baptist Historical Society** — Baptist mission records for Indian Territory
- **Individual congregations** — many records remain at the local church — **Physical**

---

### Indian Territory and tribal records *(eastern Oklahoma — parallel system)*

If your ancestor lived in eastern Oklahoma or has Five Tribes ancestry, this record system is your primary source.

- **Dawes Rolls (1898–1914):** The Dawes Commission enrolled tribal members for land allotment. Gives name, age, sex, degree of Indian blood, roll number. The *foundational* record for Five Tribes genealogy. Free on FamilySearch (within collection 1987432; 567,890 records, 283,945 images) — **Indexed + images**. Also on Ancestry and Fold3.
- **Dawes case files:** Each enrollment generated a supporting file with detailed family information — physical at **NARA at Fort Worth** (RG 75); the index is online but the files require a records request
- **Tribal census rolls (pre-Dawes):** Each tribe conducted periodic censuses through the 1890s; at NARA Fort Worth and the Oklahoma Historical Society
- **Annuity payment rolls:** Annual BIA rolls listing household heads, RG 75, M234 — **Microfilm / partial index** on FamilySearch and Fold3
- **Freedmen rolls:** Formerly enslaved people of the Five Tribes were enrolled on separate Dawes Freedmen rolls — essential for African American families in eastern Oklahoma
- **Tribal court records:** Each tribe maintained courts through 1906; physical at NARA Fort Worth and tribal archives
- **Tribal archives:** Cherokee Nation Archives (Tahlequah), Choctaw Nation (Durant), Chickasaw Nation (Ada), Muscogee Nation (Okmulgee), Seminole Nation (Wewoka)

---

### Probate and court records

- **Territorial district courts (1890–1907):** Civil cases, criminal cases, probate, naturalization. Primary repository: **National Archives at Fort Worth** (RG 21); also **Oklahoma State Archives** — **Physical only** (no comprehensive online index)
- **Indian Territory federal courts:** U.S. District Court for Indian Territory (divisions at Muskogee, McAlester, Ardmore); at **NARA at Fort Worth** — **Physical only**
- **Guardianship records:** Guardianship files for minor children are especially common in the territorial period — a critical source when a parent died without a death certificate
- **Naturalization:** Pre-1906 naturalizations were filed in any court of record. After 1906, standardized through the Bureau of Naturalization (RG 85, NARA). Search both territorial district courts and federal courts.

---

### Military records

- **Civil War pension files:** Many Land Run settlers were veterans. NARA pension files (Fold3, Ancestry) were gathered in the late 1880s–early 1900s and document the family *during the territorial period* — rich family history even though the war predated Oklahoma.
- **Spanish-American War (1898):** Oklahoma Territory volunteer units; service records at NARA (Washington); state records at the **Oklahoma Adjutant General / Oklahoma State Archives**
- **WWI draft registrations (1917–18):** Capture the settled territorial-era population as adults; indexed on FamilySearch, Ancestry, Fold3 — **Indexed + images**
- **Fold3** — comprehensive military records, subscription — **Indexed + images**

---

### Cemetery records

- **FindAGrave** ([findagrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com)) — extensive coverage of Oklahoma cemeteries, searchable by name — **Indexed + images of headstones**
- **BillionGraves** — additional coverage with GPS-linked headstone photos
- **Oklahoma Historical Society** — DAR and Oklahoma Genealogical Society cemetery survey transcriptions
- **Research note:** Many early homestead-era cemeteries were established informally on private land; some have been relocated or lost. Check newspaper obituaries as a cross-reference.

---

### Newspaper records

Oklahoma Territory had an energetic newspaper culture from Day 1 of the Land Run. Obituaries, marriage notices, land-contest notices, and probate announcements are vital-record substitutes.

- **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress) — [chroniclingamerica.loc.gov](https://chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) — free full-text search of select Oklahoma territorial papers — **Indexed full text, free**
- **Oklahoma Historical Society** — largest newspaper microfilm collection for the territory — **Microfilm, in-person**
- **Newspapers.com** — additional titles, subscription — **Indexed full text**

---

### Tax records

- County tax rolls begin with county organization (1890); annual thereafter
- Held at the **Oklahoma Historical Society** and county courthouses — **Physical / microfilm**
- Complement the 1890 territorial census and document settlers between census years; list property owners who may not appear in other records until 1900

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Collections for this place | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Early Land Records (234K records); Indian Censuses & Rolls (568K records); County Marriages (457K records); Mission & Church Records (123K records); 1900 & 1910 federal census | Free |
| **BLM GLO Records** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Land patents, homestead certificates | Free |
| **Ancestry** | Federal census (1900, 1910); Dawes Rolls; territorial newspapers; military records | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military pension files; Dawes Rolls; Indian Territory records; WWI draft | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Oklahoma cemetery records statewide | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Oklahoma territorial and early-state newspapers | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** (LOC) | Free full-text Oklahoma territorial newspapers | Free |
| **NARA Online Catalog** | Finding aids for RG 49 (land), RG 75 (Indian affairs), RG 21 (courts) | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Key holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** (Oklahoma City) | 1890 territorial census; land-run claim files; county tax rolls; newspaper microfilm; local history collections | In-person; some microfilm via interlibrary loan |
| **Oklahoma State Archives** (Oklahoma City) | Post-1908 vital records; transferred county records; school censuses; adjutant general records | In-person / mail request |
| **NARA at Fort Worth** | Territorial district court files (RG 21); Indian Territory federal courts; BIA records (RG 75) | In-person / NARA order system |
| **NARA, Washington D.C.** | Homestead case files (RG 49); naturalization (RG 85); Civil War pensions | In-person; microfilm; some via NARA catalog |
| **County courthouses** | Deed records; original marriage registers (1890+); probate files | In-person / mail |
| **Tribal archives** (5 nations) | Tribal records not in NARA; contact each nation directly | Contact tribe |
| **Presbyterian Historical Society** (Philadelphia) | Indian Territory mission records, 1820s onward | In-person / online catalog |

---

## Records NOT online

These records exist but are not yet fully available in any online database:

- **Territorial district court case files (1890–1907)** — civil, criminal, probate, naturalization — physical at NARA Fort Worth and Oklahoma State Archives; no comprehensive name index online
- **County tax rolls (1890s–1907)** — physical/microfilm at Oklahoma Historical Society; not name-indexed online
- **BIA annuity rolls** (RG 75, M234) — microfilm only; partially indexed on FamilySearch/Fold3
- **Tribal court records of the Five Civilized Tribes** — physical at NARA Fort Worth and tribal archives
- **Dawes Commission allotment case files** — the Dawes *index* is online; the individual supporting case files require a NARA Fort Worth records request
- **Individual church registers** at local congregations — not systematically microfilmed
- **School census records** — Oklahoma Historical Society; largely unindexed

---

## Known record losses and gaps

- **No territorial birth or death certificates (1889–1907):** This is a structural gap — the law never required registration. No records were destroyed; they were simply never created. Substitute sources (church, cemetery, newspaper, homestead proof) must fill this gap.
- **1890 federal census:** Largely destroyed in a 1921 fire and subsequent 1934 congressional authorization for disposal. Oklahoma Territory is one of the rare exceptions — the separate 1890 territorial census survived.
- **Civil War damage to Indian Territory records:** Many tribal records from the 1860s were lost or destroyed as tribal communities were displaced during and after the Civil War.
- **Early county record-keeping:** Counties established immediately after land runs sometimes had informal or incomplete record-keeping in their first months of existence.
- **Rural church records:** Congregations meeting in homes or temporary structures often left no formal registers; what records exist may remain in private hands.

---

## Research tips

1. **Land records first.** The homestead entry file at NARA (RG 49) is the single most important document for a Land Run family. Start at [glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov) to find the patent, then request the full case file from NARA for witness names and family details.

2. **Know your territory.** Confirm whether your ancestor's county was in Oklahoma Territory or Indian Territory before searching — the record systems are completely separate.

3. **Use the 1890 territorial census.** It is one of the only 1890 enumerations surviving anywhere in the country. Check the Oklahoma Historical Society's holdings and FamilySearch.

4. **Search origin-state records.** Most Land Run settlers came from Kansas, Texas, Missouri, and Arkansas. Birth, marriage, church, and land records in those states document the family *before* the run and supply the maiden names and birthplaces that territorial records omit.

5. **Homestead witnesses are FAN-cluster gold.** The two required witnesses to a homestead proof were typically neighbors or relatives — often from the same origin county back home. Follow them to find extended family.

6. **For Five Tribes ancestry, start with the Dawes Rolls** (free on FamilySearch). The roll number connects to the individual allotment case file at NARA Fort Worth, which contains detailed family information.

7. **Freedmen rolls are a separate Dawes series.** African American families who were freedmen of the Five Tribes appear on dedicated Freedmen rolls within the Dawes Commission records — do not overlook these when researching Black families in eastern Oklahoma.

8. **Newspapers substitute for vital records.** The Oklahoma Historical Society's microfilm collection and Chronicling America are the best access points. Obituaries, marriage notices, and probate announcements appear in territorial weeklies from 1889.

9. **Civil War pension files document the territorial period.** Many Land Run settlers were veterans whose pension files were compiled in the late 1880s–early 1900s. These NARA files often name wives, children, and neighbors in detail — directly covering the Land Run era.

10. **County formation dates affect your search.** If a county didn't exist at your target event date, look to the parent land office (Guthrie or Kingfisher) or the parent county for records.

---

(4) Search both MDAH (Jackson) and ADAH for territorial authority records. (5) For Mobile: work backwards through French/British/Spanish colonial records via Catholic registers. (6) Tax lists as annual census substitute. (7) Pension files for military conflicts. (8) FamilySearch Catalog for microfilm-only volumes. Includes specific pitfalls: pre-1810 records sparse; Washington County records incomplete; pre-1813 Mobile records dispersed; interior pre-Creek War has no American civil records. Population context (1,250 settlers c. 1800) provided to manage expectations.
