# Locality Guide: Alabama Region — Pre-Statehood Era (c. 1780–1819)

> **Jurisdictional note:** Alabama did not exist as a jurisdiction in 1800. The entire settled region was **Washington County, Mississippi Territory** — and before 1798 it was variously Spanish, British, or French colonial territory. Everything below flows from that reality.

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

| Factor | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Governing jurisdiction, c. 1800** | Washington County, Mississippi Territory |
| **Mississippi Territory created** | 1798, from Georgia land cessions and Native treaty cessions |
| **Alabama Territory created** | 1817 (split from Mississippi Territory) |
| **Statehood** | December 14, 1819 |
| **Administrative center, c. 1800** | St. Stephens, Washington County (Tombigbee River) |
| **Population, c. 1800** | ~1,250 non-Native settlers in the Alabama portion |
| **Population, 1810 federal census** | ~9,046 in the Alabama portion of Mississippi Territory |
| **Population, 1820 (first state census)** | 127,901 |
| **Economy** | Subsistence and commercial agriculture (cotton emerging), river trade |
| **Ethnic groups** | English, Scots-Irish, French Creole (Mobile), Spanish Creole (Mobile), free and enslaved African Americans, Creek and Cherokee nations (interior) |
| **Religious denominations** | Methodist circuit riders (from c. 1808), Baptist (c. 1808–1810 in north Alabama), Catholic (Mobile, continuously from the French era, 1702) |
| **Key events** | Creek War 1813–14 (opened interior to settlement); U.S. seizure of Mobile from Spain 1813; land rush 1817–1819 preceding statehood |

---

## Boundary Changes and County Formation

**The most critical research hurdle.** Every record follows the county that created it, not today's county lines.

| County | Formed | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Washington County** | 1800 | Covered virtually all of present-day Alabama; county seat St. Stephens |
| **Madison County** | 1808 | Northern Alabama (Huntsville area) |
| **Baldwin County** | 1809 | Southwest Alabama, Mobile Bay area |
| **Mobile County** | 1812 | Created after U.S. seized Mobile from Spain |
| **Limestone, Lawrence, Montgomery-area counties** | 1816–1818 | Multiple counties formed just before statehood |

**Research implication:** For any event before 1808, nearly all American-era civil records for present-day Alabama will be found in **Washington County** records. For events in or near Mobile before 1813, look first to **Spanish colonial** then to Mobile/Baldwin County records.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)

- **Alabama had no statewide civil registration until 1908.** No systematic birth or death records exist for the territorial period.
- **Marriage registers** exist at the county level from formation date:
  - Washington County: from **1800** (oldest in Alabama)
  - Madison County: from **1808** | Baldwin: **1809** | Mobile: **1812**
- **FamilySearch:** *Alabama, County Marriages, 1809–1950* (Collection 1598408) — 612,345 indexed records, 306,172 images — **Indexed + images** — though the earliest Washington County years (1800–1808) may not be fully represented.
- **Alternatives for vital events:** Catholic church registers (Mobile, from 1702); Methodist/Baptist registers (from c. 1808–1810, but very sparse); probate inventories naming deceased and heirs.

### Church Records

- **Catholic (Mobile)** — The most important early church records in the region. The Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception in Mobile maintained registers of baptisms, marriages, and burials from the **French colonial period (1702)** continuously through British, Spanish, and American eras. These are the oldest surviving genealogical records for any part of Alabama.
  - Spanish-era Catholic records: **Archivo General de Indias (AGI), Seville** — *Papeles de Cuba* series — with microfilm copies at the **Library of Congress** and the **Archdiocese of Mobile Archives**.
  - French colonial church records: **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM), Aix-en-Provence, France**, with some microfilm at the **Mobile Municipal Archives**.
- **Protestant (frontier, from c. 1808):** Methodist and Baptist records are rare and scattered before 1820.
- **FamilySearch:** *Alabama, Church Records, 1803–1950* (Collection 1986396) — 156,789 indexed records, 78,394 images — **Indexed + images** — pre-1820 coverage is thin.
- **Language note:** French colonial records are in **French**; Spanish colonial records in **Spanish**; sacramental Latin entries appear throughout.

### Census and Enumeration Records

| Census | Year(s) | Coverage | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mississippi Territory enumeration | 1801, 1805, 1808, 1816 | Alabama portion of territory | Physical / microfilm at ADAH; some compiled in indexed collections |
| **U.S. Federal Census (Mississippi Territory)** | **1810** | First federal census covering Alabama (Washington, Madison, Baldwin counties) | Indexed + images on FamilySearch and Ancestry |
| Spanish colonial census (*padrón*) | Various, 1780s–1812 | Mobile area only | AGI, Seville; LOC microfilm copies |
| FamilySearch compiled index — Alabama | 1810–1890 | Compiled census substitutes | Collection 2016756 — 287,634 records — **index only, no images** |
| FamilySearch compiled index — Mississippi/Territory | 1792–1890 | Includes territorial period | Collection 1927171 — 198,340 records — **index only, no images** |

- The **1810 federal census** names heads of household only. It is the only federal census covering the Alabama region before statehood.
- **Territorial enumerations (1801, 1805, 1808, 1816)** sometimes record more household information. Held at ADAH, Montgomery.
- **Tax lists** (see below) function as annual census substitutes throughout this period.

### Land Records — The Single Most Important Category

Alabama is a **federal land state**. The U.S. government surveyed and sold public land directly to settlers after Native cessions. This generated a rich, well-preserved, and **fully digitized** record trail.

**Federal Land Patents (from 1803):**
- Two land offices served the Alabama region: **St. Stephens** (southwest Alabama, from 1803) and **Huntsville** (north Alabama, from 1809).
- Original entry files at **NARA, Record Group 49**.
- ★ **The BLM General Land Office website ([glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov)) has digitized ALL Alabama federal land patents.** Records are fully name-indexed; original patent documents and survey plats are downloadable as images. **Free.** — **Indexed + images.** This is the most comprehensively digitized pre-statehood record set for Alabama.

**Spanish Land Grants (Mobile district, pre-1813):**
- Holders of Spanish grants had to register and confirm their titles in American courts after 1813. Confirmed grants appear in Mobile and Baldwin County deed books and territorial court records.
- Original documentation: AGI Seville (*Papeles de Cuba*); microfilm at LOC and ADAH.

**County Deed Records:**
- Washington County deed books from 1800: **Washington County Courthouse, Chatom, AL**; some volumes microfilmed.
- **FamilySearch:** *Mississippi, Territorial and State Land Records, 1798–1907* (Collection 1919694) — 312,450 indexed records, 156,225 images — **Indexed + images** — though catalogued under Mississippi, this collection covers the Mississippi Territory as a whole, including the Alabama portion, and is highly relevant for 1798–1819.

### Probate and Court Records

- **Washington County probate** begins from 1800, though records before 1810 are sparse: **Washington County Courthouse, Chatom**.
- **Territorial superior court** records at **ADAH, Montgomery** — civil cases, criminal proceedings, some naturalization-like filings.
- **FamilySearch:** *Alabama, Probate Records, 1809–1985* (Collection 1537764) — 438,210 indexed records, 219,105 images — **Indexed + images**.
- **FamilySearch:** *Mississippi, County Court Records, 1799–1920* (Collection 1855293) — 245,670 indexed records, 122,835 images — **Indexed + images** (includes territorial-era records).

### Military Records

| Conflict | Years | Record types | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Territorial militia | 1798–1817 | Muster rolls, rosters | ADAH; some at NARA |
| War of 1812 | 1812–1815 | Service records, pensions, bounty land warrants | NARA RG 94; Fold3 (indexed + images) |
| Creek War | 1813–1814 | Service records, pension files | NARA RG 94; Fold3 (indexed + images) |

**Pension files** are genealogically the richest military record type, often naming family members, giving birth states and dates, and containing widow's declarations with marriage information. Cross-reference pension land warrants with BLM GLO to find the veteran's land tract.

### Tax Records

- Territorial tax lists were compiled annually from the early 1800s and survive in fragmentary form.
- **Critical substitute:** Because there is no census before 1810 and the 1810 census names only heads of household, **annual tax lists (1800–1817)** are often the only record placing a specific person in the territory year by year.
- Held at **ADAH, Montgomery** and **MDAH, Jackson** (records predating the 1817 territorial split).
- Some have been transcribed by the Alabama Genealogical Society and appear in published journals.
- **Access: microfilm or physical at ADAH/MDAH; not available in any online searchable database.**

### Colonial Records (Mobile District — Spanish and French)

Essential for any family with Mobile-area roots.

| Colonial power | Period | Record types | Primary repository |
|---|---|---|---|
| **French** | 1702–1763 | Church registers, land concessions, notarial records | ANOM, Aix-en-Provence; microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives |
| **British** | 1763–1780 | Land grants, military returns, loyalist claims | The National Archives, Kew, UK |
| **Spanish** | 1780–1813 | Census (*padrones*), church registers, land grants, notarial records, civil court records | AGI, Seville (*Papeles de Cuba*); copies at LOC and ADAH |

---

## Digitized Browsable Volumes — Your Specific Question

You asked specifically about digitized volumes that can be browsed even without a name index. Here is the honest picture:

**FamilySearch's Records Management Service (volume search) returned zero results for both Alabama and Mississippi Territory in the 1780–1819 window.** This is a cataloguing/digitization gap in FamilySearch's RMS — it does not mean no digitized records exist. The FamilySearch collections (which use a different system) do have images.

**Where digitized browsable records actually exist:**

| Resource | What's browsable | Name index? | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BLM GLO** ([glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov)) | All Alabama federal land patents from 1803; survey plats and patent images | ✅ Fully indexed | Free |
| **FamilySearch Coll. 1598408** — AL County Marriages, 1809–1950 | 306,172 marriage register images | ✅ Indexed | Free |
| **FamilySearch Coll. 1537764** — AL Probate Records, 1809–1985 | 219,105 probate volume images | ✅ Indexed | Free |
| **FamilySearch Coll. 1986396** — AL Church Records, 1803–1950 | 78,394 images | ✅ Indexed | Free |
| **FamilySearch Coll. 1919694** — MS Territorial and State Land Records, 1798–1907 | 156,225 images (covers AL territorial period) | ✅ Indexed | Free |
| **FamilySearch Coll. 1855293** — MS County Court Records, 1799–1920 | 122,835 images | ✅ Indexed | Free |
| **Fold3** | War of 1812 / Creek War service records and pensions | ✅ Indexed | Subscription |
| **Ancestry** | 1810 federal census (MS Territory) images | ✅ Indexed | Subscription |
| **ADAH Digital Collections** ([adah.alabama.gov](https://adah.alabama.gov)) | Select digitized territorial materials; browsable | Partial | Free |

**Browse-only (images with no name index):** The FamilySearch RMS returned no unindexed volumes for this period. Any truly browse-only volumes that exist are in physical microfilm collections (see below). The BLM GLO and FamilySearch collections listed above are all indexed. The compiled census substitute collections (1927171, 2016756) are the opposite problem — index only, **no images**.

---

## Records NOT Online

The following record types exist for this period but are not in any online name-searchable database:

- **Territorial tax lists (1800–1817)** — ADAH, microfilm; not digitized or indexed online
- **Washington County court minutes (1800–1808)** — Washington County Courthouse; partially microfilmed at ADAH
- **Territorial superior court records** — ADAH; not digitized
- **Spanish colonial census (*padrón*) returns for Mobile district** — AGI, Seville; some microfilmed at LOC; not online in searchable form
- **French colonial notarial and land concession records** — ANOM; not online
- **Early Methodist and Baptist circuit records (pre-1815)** — Scattered in denominational archives; not systematically digitized
- **British colonial land grants (Mobile, 1763–1780)** — The National Archives, Kew; not online for this area

---

## Known Record Losses

- **Washington County records (1800–1810):** Administrative records from the frontier era are incomplete; some permanent losses from early courthouse conditions.
- **Pre-1813 Mobile civil records:** Spanish colonial civil records held locally in Mobile were largely dispersed when the U.S. took Mobile in 1813. Surviving originals are at AGI, Seville.
- **Interior Alabama (pre-Creek War):** The territory north and east of the Tombigbee settlements was Indian country with no American civil record creation before 1814–1817. No American substitute sources exist for families living outside the narrow Tombigbee corridor before that date.
- **1810 federal census limitations:** Lists only heads of household; does not name wives, children, or other household members.

**Partial compensations for losses:**
- BLM GLO patents document settlers from the moment they entered public land (1803 onward)
- Territorial tax lists at ADAH give annual individual-level presence
- Catholic church registers (Mobile) provide continuous individual documentation from 1702

---

## Repository Guide

### Online

| Repository | Collections for this place/period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **BLM GLO** ([glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov)) | All Alabama federal land patents from 1803 | Free |
| **FamilySearch** ([familysearch.org](https://familysearch.org)) | County marriages, probate, church records, MS territorial land and court records, compiled census substitutes | Free |
| **ADAH Digital Collections** ([adah.alabama.gov](https://adah.alabama.gov)) | Select territorial records, browsable | Free |
| **Ancestry** ([ancestry.com](https://ancestry.com)) | 1810 MS Territory federal census; some compiled territorial records | Subscription |
| **Fold3** ([fold3.com](https://fold3.com)) | War of 1812 and Creek War service records, pensions | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** ([loc.gov](https://www.loc.gov/collections/chronicling-america)) | Very limited pre-statehood Alabama newspapers | Free |

### Physical

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Alabama Dept. of Archives and History (ADAH)**, Montgomery, AL | Territorial records; tax lists; militia rolls; territorial censuses; county records on microfilm | In-person; adah.alabama.gov |
| **Mississippi Dept. of Archives and History (MDAH)**, Jackson, MS | Records created under the Mississippi Territory (pre-1817) including Alabama district | In-person; mdah.ms.gov |
| **NARA Southeast Region**, Atlanta, GA | Federal court and land records; RG 49 (land), RG 94 (military) | In-person or written request |
| **Washington County Courthouse**, Chatom, AL | Deed books, probate minutes, marriage registers from 1800 | In-person or written inquiry |
| **Mobile Municipal Archives**, Mobile, AL | Mobile County records from 1812; French colonial microfilm | In-person |
| **Archdiocese of Mobile Archives**, Mobile, AL | Catholic registers from 1702 onward | By appointment |
| **Library of Congress**, Washington, DC | Microfilm of Spanish colonial records (AGI *Papeles de Cuba*) | In-person; some online at loc.gov |
| **Archivo General de Indias (AGI)**, Seville, Spain | Original Spanish colonial records for Mobile district | In-person; some microfilmed |
| **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)**, Aix-en-Provence, France | French colonial records (1702–1763) | In-person; some microfilmed |

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with BLM GLO.** For any ancestor who settled in Alabama before statehood, the federal land patent is often the first American document that places them in the territory — and the entire database is free, indexed, and image-accessible at [glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov).

2. **Identify the correct county at the event date.** Before 1808, the answer is almost always **Washington County**. Use the Newberry Library's *Atlas of Historical County Boundaries* ([publications.newberry.org/ahcbp](https://publications.newberry.org/ahcbp)) to verify.

3. **The Mississippi Territory is the parent jurisdiction.** Records created under territorial authority may be at **MDAH, Jackson** as well as ADAH, Montgomery. Search both.

4. **For Mobile-area families, work backwards through three colonial systems.** The Catholic church registers at Mobile are the thread connecting the French (1702), British (1763), Spanish (1780), and American (1813) periods. The FamilySearch Wiki article *Alabama Genealogy* ([familysearch.org/en/wiki/Alabama_Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Alabama_Genealogy)) outlines the chain.

5. **Tax lists are your annual census.** The only federal census before statehood is 1810 (heads of household only). Territorial tax lists at ADAH are the primary tool for tracking individuals year by year before that.

6. **Creek War and War of 1812 pension files are genealogically rich.** Pension applications often contain the veteran's birth state and year, a service narrative, and a widow's declaration that names children and gives a marriage date and place. Fold3 has these indexed and imaged.

7. **The FamilySearch Catalog is different from Collections Search.** Search the catalog at [familysearch.org/catalog](https://www.familysearch.org/catalog) by place = "Alabama" and "Washington County, Alabama" to find microfilmed volumes not yet digitized or indexed. Many early county records were filmed but remain browse-only on microfilm.

8. **Population context matters.** Around 1800 only ~1,250 non-Native settlers lived in all of present-day Alabama. The record corpus is correspondingly small. Most ancestors in this region arrived during the 1817–1819 land rush. A missing ancestor before 1817 may simply not yet have moved to Alabama.

---

*Guide saved to `alabama-pre-statehood-locality-guide.md`. Sources: FamilySearch Wiki articles "Alabama Genealogy" and "Mississippi Territory Genealogy"; FamilySearch collections search (Alabama and Mississippi Territory, 1780–1819); FamilySearch volume search (returned 0 results for this period — noted as a coverage gap); BLM GLO documentation; Wikipedia summary for Alabama. Guide produced June 2026.*
